coredata.dc:title: Recent advances on rapid detection and remediation of environmental pollutants utilizing nanomaterials-based (bio)sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Abhinay
Kumar, Ashish
coredata.dc:description: Environmental safety has become a significant issue for the safety of living species, humans, and the ecosystem as a consequence of the harmful and detrimental consequences of various pollutants such as pesticides, heavy metals, dyes, etc., emitted into the surroundings. To resolve this issue, various efforts, legal acts, scientific and technological perspectives have been embraced, but still remain a global concern. Furthermore, due to non-portability, complex detection, and inappropriate on-site recognition of sophisticated laboratory tools, the real-time analysis of these environmental contaminants has been limited. As a result of innovative nano bioconjugation and nanofabrication techniques, nanotechnology enables enhanced nanomaterials (NMs) based (bio)sensors demonstrating ultra-sensitivity and a short detection time in real-time analysis, as well as superior sensitivity, reliability, and selectivity have been developed. Several researchers have demonstrated the potent detection of pollutants such as Hg2+ ion by the usage of AgNP-MD in electronic and optoelectronic methods with a detection limit of 5–45 μM which is quite significant. Taking into consideration of such tremendous research, herein, the authors have highlighted 21st-century strategies towards NMs based biosensor technology for pollutants detection, including nano biosensors, enzyme-based biosensors, electrochemical-based biosensors, carbon-based biosensors and optical biosensors for on-site identification and detection of target analytes. This article will provide a brief overview of the significance of utilizing NMs-based biosensors for the detection of a diverse array of hazardous pollutants, and a thorough understanding of the detection processes of NMs-based biosensors, as well as the limit of quantification (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) values, rendering researchers to focus on the world's need for a sustainable earth.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental safety has become a significant issue for the safety of living species, humans, and the ecosystem as a consequence of the harmful and detrimental consequences of various pollutants such as pesticides, heavy metals, dyes, etc., emitted into the surroundings. To resolve this issue, various efforts, legal acts, scientific and technological perspectives have been embraced, but still remain a global concern. Furthermore, due to non-portability, complex detection, and inappropriate on-site recognition of sophisticated laboratory tools, the real-time analysis of these environmental contaminants has been limited. As a result of innovative nano bioconjugation and nanofabrication techniques, nanotechnology enables enhanced nanomaterials (NMs) based (bio)sensors demonstrating ultra-sensitivity and a short detection time in real-time analysis, as well as superior sensitivity, reliability, and selectivity have been developed. Several researchers have demonstrated the potent detection of pollutants such as Hg2+ ion by the usage of AgNP-MD in electronic and optoelectronic methods with a detection limit of 5–45 μM which is quite significant. Taking into consideration of such tremendous research, herein, the authors have highlighted 21st-century strategies towards NMs based biosensor technology for pollutants detection, including nano biosensors, enzyme-based biosensors, electrochemical-based biosensors, carbon-based biosensors and optical biosensors for on-site identification and detection of target analytes. This article will provide a brief overview of the significance of utilizing NMs-based biosensors for the detection of a diverse array of hazardous pollutants, and a thorough understanding of the detection processes of NMs-based biosensors, as well as the limit of quantification (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) values, rendering researchers to focus on the world's need for a sustainable earth.

ce:section.ce:para: The exceeded utilization of pesticides, dyes, fertilizers and herbicides, and improper disposal of such pollutants and non-biodegradable substances have increased particulate matter (PM) pollution in our surroundings as well as carbon dioxide (CO2) and other greenhouse gas pollution in the atmosphere from a multitude of chemicals, oil spills and pollutants (Kalyani et al., 2021; Srivastava et al., 2021). Emerging pollutants (EP), which include a broad spectrum of chemicals such as pesticides, dyes, pharmaceuticals waste, etc., cause a slew of issues when it comes to removing them from the ecosystem. For instance, when treating wastewater containing emerging pollutants, it is crucial to consider both the interrelations between pollutants and the interrelations among contaminants and numerous ecological terms like season successors, solar radiation intensity, temperature and hydraulic resistances (Brooks and Alper, 2021; Khoshbin et al., 2021; Sciuto et al., 2021; Stanciu et al., 2021). EPs can enter the ecosystem via a wide range of node or diffuse sources including industries, homes, hospitals, lands; afterward transmit to the soil, atmosphere, or waterways via a wide assortment of pathways or mechanisms primarily dictated by EP attributes (polarity, variability, persistence, and so on) and ecologic enclosure attributes; where insufficiently processed discharges and improper disposal of such pollutants, EPs readily become contaminants of our ecosystems. For instance, considering the EPs transmit into the water ecosystem, they may (bio)accumulate in sediment and river fauna and flora as a result of their persistence, as chemical degradation, biodegradation and photolysis (in the lack of light) all proceed at quite minimal levels. As a result, fauna and flora may be nearly extinct from the aquatic ecology. As EPs have the ability to enter the food chain, it is critical to enhance research effort and resources dedicated to examining, detecting, remediation and all alternatives for minimizing the consequences and hazards of EPs, as well as the resulting health effects (Aykaç et al., 2021; Pilevar et al., 2021). EPs exist in various forms, some of them are:

To prevent the health and the environment from the proliferation of industrial and natural inorganic and organic toxins, hazardous pollutants must be monitored in the soil, air and water. The demand for online monitoring of toxins in our environment is growing as a result of new rules and developments. Controlling wastewater quality to prevent the discharge of synthetic pharmaceutical residues or surfactants, pesticides, as well as regulating carcinogens or explosive residues discharged into the atmosphere, are two examples (Amin et al., 2020; Raymundo-Pereira et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2020). In industry and agriculture, pesticides (herbicides, fungicides, and insecticides) are commonly employed. Pesticides are chemicals used in the production, processing, transportation, and commercialization of foods, agriculture products, livestock feed, and items that could be fed to animals to regulate insects, pests and bacteria. As a result, pesticides are crucial in agriculture, allowing for huge improvements in yields by removing and controlling pests. In a study, it is revealed that pesticide use has increased considerably as the world's population has grown, with Europe accounting for 45% of pesticide use, the United States (US) for 25%, and 25% for the remaining of the globe (Sharma et al., 2019). Herbicides tends for 47.5%, insecticides for 29.5%, fungicides for 17.5%, and other pesticides for 5.5%. China, Argentina, Thailand, the United States, Brazil, Italy, Japan, France, Canada and India are among the world's top 10 pesticide-using countries on the earth (Smith, 2021). Pesticides usually persist in water, food, and the ecosystem instead of instantly affecting the target species. Human health is jeopardized by this unintended accumulation. The active ingredients in pesticide formulations can lead to anxiety, agitation, convulsions, serious neurological disorders, and malignant melanoma. Furthermore, because the quantity of these harmful substances is growing significantly, it disrupts the ecological balance. Pesticide contamination primarily affects workers in pesticide manufacturing firms, in fields, in the extermination of residential pests and greenhouses. As a result of these factors, efforts to measure and quantify pesticides and several other EPs in the ecosystem are ongoing.

Another type of EP is heavy metals and their contaminations naturally occur in the ecosystem. However, as a consequence of industrialization, massive quantities of heavy metals obligated in minerals and fossil fuels have been discharged into the surrounding and have accumulated in minute amounts in almost every corner of the globe. The presence of massive levels of heavy metals in groundwater could be hazardous to human health as well as the ecosystem. According to recent world healthcare organization (WHO) studies, over 1 billion individuals lack access to clean and drinkable water, and 1.6 million individuals die every year as a consequence of biological and non-biological toxins in water (Gaviria-Arroyave et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Nagar and Pradeep, 2020; Tseng et al., 2020). Surface waters, such as worldwide rivers and lakes, are appropriate test subjects for determining global heavy metal pollution levels. The threshold concentrations of these metals are also displayed in accordance with the United states environmental protection agency (USEPA) and WHO guidelines. The mean heavy metal concentration has been increasing since the 1990s. There are several types of heavy metals that have adverse effects on the ecosystem and human health such as arsenic (As) which is a hazardous and malignant metalloid with a broad dispersion in the ecosystem, creating many ecological issues in personal and public health around the entire globe. Arsenic is found in a wide range of meals, including cereals and aquaculture. As these substances are consumed by humans and animals and accumulate in biological systems, cause lethal impacts on the body and lifestyle. Agricultural businesses, such as pesticide production, expose workers to high quantities of arsenic. Metal smelting businesses, particularly copper smelting, are also significant contributors to pollution due to dust and arsenic fumes. The main form of arsenic contamination in the environment is drinking water. According to a recent study, arsenic-contaminated water (10 ng/mL) is consumed by 150,000 people on a regular basis (Filik and Avan, 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Chronic long-term interaction to low levels of As has been linked to the development of critical diseases such as cardiovascular disease, high blood pressure, diabetes, keratosis, liver failure, stomach flu, nerve damage, encephalitis, Pancytopenia, and a variety of cancers, according to experimental and epidemiological research (Garcia-Segura et al., 2020). Another heavy metal is lead (Pb) whose pervasive usage in the industry has resulted in pollution of the environment, soil, and groundwater. It is not biodegradable since it is a non-essential component of the human body, and its ecotoxicity has remained a source of apprehension despite warnings from worldwide health organizations. Water and food are the most common sources of lead poisoning, while occupational exposure is produced by inhaling and swallowing lead-containing gases and dirt (George et al., 2020; Lochab et al., 2020; Yi et al., 2020). The WHO has established the permissible level of lead in drinkable water at 0.01 g/L. In erythrocytes, hemoglobin binds 99% of the blood lead. Lead is a chronic pollutant in the environment and biological system that can cause a range of health problems in humans, ranging from acute to chronic poisoning. Chronic poisoning causes chromosomal abnormalities, mental impairment, respiratory problems, autistic, impulsivity, organ failure, extreme muscle spasms, disorder, and death, whereas acute poisoning induces migraines, abdominal discomfort, hypertension, kidney dysfunction, drowsiness, paranoid delusions, hypersomnia, and osteoarthritis (Dhiman et al., 2020; Mathew, 2020; Pérez-Fernández et al., 2020). Another example of heavy metal is mercury (Hg) which is a heavy metal that is a great representation of metal's environmentalism. Rainwater contains an inorganic form of mercury oxide that helps to restore mercury to the surface of the earth. Mercury is introduced into the aquatic food chain through the methylation process, and accumulated in tissues are 1800 to 8000 times greater than in the water column (Gopinath et al., 2020; Jepson et al., 2020; Khan et al., 2020; Li and Row, 2020; Rastogi et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). Humans are subjected to numerous aspects of mercury in a variety of ways, including accidental exposure, food and environmental contamination, dental care and amalgam fabrication, pharmaceutical product retention, occupational pollutants in the electronics sector, valuable metal manufacturing, caustic soda, and antifungal generation. Methylmercury and mercury are both easily absorbed substances. The epithelial cells and lungs quickly absorb mercury vapor, which is a lipophilic material. When mercury enters the bloodstream, it is oxidized to Hg2+, a highly reactive metal. Mercury toxicity manifests in the gastrointestinal system, neurological system, and kidneys because it is absorbed in substantial amounts by the hepatic, renal, and nerve tissue. As a result, the implementation of health legislation in developed countries to reduce worldwide heavy metal contamination has resulted in comparative regulation of such pollutants (Chen et al., 2020; Fethi, 2020; Wang et al., 2020).

As mentioned above, the number of EPs in developing countries is rapidly increasing which led to various fatal diseases such as cancer which is a global topic that is constantly engaging researchers from all around the globe. EPs exposure increases the incidence of malignancies of the prostate, chest, kidney, liver, intestines, leukemia, and myelodysplastic syndrome, according to evidence from an agricultural health study. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) assessed the congenic risks of pesticides to human beings, which is widely used to identify ecological carcinogens and assist authorities in formulating policies to protect the community from the hazard of cancer caused by meals, the eco system, and working cancer-causing intermediaries. Another lethal disease is diabetes, whose risk can increase when people are exposed to pesticides on a regular basis. There is a link between organochlorine chemicals and diabetes, and a comparable link has been found between organophosphate and the incidence of type 2 diabetes. The link between pesticide contact and the prevalence of gestational diabetes was explored in a study of 11,273 women, and it was discovered that residential women who used pesticides mostly in the yard and at the house seemed to have no risk of gestational diabetes throughout their pregnancies (Alharbi et al., 2020; Ba Hashwan et al., 2020; Christopher et al., 2020; Jiaojiao et al., 2020). Whereas, females who worked in agriculture and were exposed to pesticides and herbicides on a regular basis had the highest chance of gestational diabetes during pregnancy. The exposure of EPs can also led to the arisen of respiratory disorders. Proximity to several EPs such as chemicals, organic dust and hazardous gases could cause lung disorders in farm workers, such as bronchitis, asthma, silo filler's disorder, organic dust deadly condition, over-sensitivity pneumonitis and neuromuscular respiratory damage. Numerous research has suggested that there is a link among asthma and pesticide exposure. EPs could led to asthma symptoms such as irritation, edoema, endocrine disruption, and immunological suppression. For instance, research evidence demonstrated that agricultural pesticide exposure was linked to higher rates of lung cancer, especially when exposure lasted more than 2 days per month. Also, organophosphates may play an impact on respiratory problems, according to a study of wheezing in farmers and commercial applicators (Gai et al., 2019b; Guo et al., 2019; Kalambate et al., 2019). In a research, Bexfield et al. (2021) performed the initial examination of groundwater through public-supply wells around the US to screen for over 100 pesticide defernites and provide a human-health viewpoint for those that do not have standards. In samples from 1204 wells in aquifers, 116 degradates and 109 pesticides (active chemicals) were discovered, accounting for 70% of the amount drawn for drinking water. Compound combinations made up over two-thirds of the 41% of wells with pesticide chemicals, and degradates made up three-quarters. Atrazine, tebuthiuron, hexazinone, prometon and one metolachlor degradation were found in more than 5% of wells.

Hence, several recent developments in detection technology for some EPs have resulted in the detection of a growing number of pollutants and their conversion byproducts in previously unknown sources. For instance, several microbial communities have the ability to counteract EPs biocide activity and maybe modify them, accelerating up the process of disintegration (Antiochia, 2021; Dutta et al., 2021; Garg et al., 2021; Maghsoudi et al., 2021; Polshettiwar et al., 2021). The utilization of microbes and their associated distinctive enzymatic activities to break down, decompose, detox, or alter environmental contaminants is referred to as microbial degradation. For example, the bioluminescence gene lux, which has been cloned in Photorhabdus luminescens and Vibrio fischeri, encodes for the luciferase enzyme, which catalyzes the light-emitting process and functions as a light reporter gene (Meighen, 1991). This gene was frequently employed in the production of whole-cell microbial biosensors for environmental pollution monitoring. Bacterial luciferase produces blue-green light by catalyzing the oxidation of a long-chain fatty aldehyde in the context of molecular oxygen. Using sensitive instruments such as fiber optic probes and integral circuit chips to monitor generated light, proved successful in identifying contaminants. However, under limited growth conditions for microbes, effective degradation of contaminants is not expected now, as microbes with degradation capabilities exist in the environment. Also, this research is generally conducted in laboratories, due to which there is no assurance that the experimental system adequately represents real-world applications, as well as the wide relevance of these discoveries, is not yet evident. Thereby, controlling environmental contamination and EPs has become a difficult issue because of the increased mobility and globalization of utilization of pesticides, fertilizers, dyes, pharmaceuticals which results in the rise of EPs and contamination of our motherland. In research, Jepson et al. (2020) categorized 659 pesticides into two categories: acute and chronic human health hazards (e.g., respiratory and carcinogenic impacts) and environmental concerns (e.g., biomagnification and ozone depletion, as well as threats to aquatic life, terrestrial species, and pollinators). Similarly, more than a million agricultural farmers in the tropical and US integrated pest management (IPM) programs have examined the categorization of quite hazardous and high-risk EPs. For monitoring EPs in environmental samples, two distinct methodologies were proposed. The conventional strategy enables reliable examination of chemical or physical features of environmental samples, but it necessitates the purchase of expensive analytical tools and the use of specialized laboratories to analyze toxins in the ecosystem (Ali et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020; Quinchia et al., 2020; Santiago, 2020; Ul Amin et al., 2020). Although several analytical tools such as gas/liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), ultraviolet-vis spectroscopy (UV), fluorescence (FL) detection and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) are highly delicate and quantified, requiring prolonged specimen preparation or complicated apparatus, making them time-consuming. Furthermore, these equipment are costly, and can only be used by those with a high grade of knowledge.

Owing to the aforementioned limitations, real-time, online analysis of chemical entity detection with excellent sensitivity and spatial precision is essential. Taking into consideration, the utilization of NMs-based biosensors has emerged to be the real-time sample analysis of the wide range of EPs in a precise and quantified manner. Before proceeding further, we should be aware of the design and working principle of a biosensor. A biosensor is a tool or detector which produces a detectable signal by combining a biological component such as an enzyme or an antibody plus an electrical element. The electronic element monitors, stores, and transmits information on physiological changes as well as the presence of a wide range of biological or chemical materials in the environment. Biosensors are available in a wide range of shapes and sizes, and they can detect and measure even minute amounts of diseases, hazardous chemicals, and the potential of hydrogen (pH) levels.
(a)
Analyte: An interesting material whose components are being recognized or identified (e.g., ammonia, glucose, lactose and alcohol).
(b)
Bioreceptor: Bioreceptors (e.g., cells, enzymes, aptamers, antibodies, deoxyribonucleic acid, or ribonucleic acid (DNA or RNA)) are biomolecules (molecules) or biological elements that could detect the target substrate (i.e., analyte). Throughout the interaction involving analyte and bioreceptor, signal generation is termed biorecognition.
(c)
Transducer: A equipment that converts one form of energy to another is known as an energy converter. The transducer is an integral part of a biosensor. It transforms a biorecognition phenomenon into an electrical signal that represents an amount or indicates the inclusion of a biological or chemical target. Such energy transformation method is termed as signalization. The number of electrical or optical signals created by transducers determines the number of analytes–bioreceptor interactions. Based on their functioning mechanism, transducers are categorized as electrochemical, thermal, optical, gravimetric, or electronic transducers.
(d)
Electronics: After the signal has been transduced, it is evaluated and processed for projection. The electrical impulses from the transducer are boosted and transformed to digital format. The display unit quantifies the signals that have been processed.
(e)
Display: The output is provided by a user interpretation system, which includes a printer or computer, so that the person may read and grasp the relevant response. The output could be in the format of a numerical, cartographic, or statistical value, or a graphic, depending on the end-user requirement. A standard biosensor includes (a) an analyte, (b) bioreceptor, (c) transducer, (d) electronics, and (e) display (Fig. 1 ).

Aside from the bioreceptors listed above, NMs have subsequently been identified as a novel type of bioreceptor. Various NMs have been utilized as bioreceptors as nanoscience and nanotechnology have progressed. In biosensing science, nanoparticles (NPs) have a wider variety of uses. NMs could function as both transducers and bioreceptors. NMs are also appealing due to their distinct physico-chemical characteristics. NMs can be utilized to construct novel biosensor systems with increased sensitivity since they have significant surface area to volume ratios and could be adjusted to control electrode kinetics because they have a nanoscale dimension. On their utilization, the study on trace detecting and treatment of pollutants, etc. has made extended progress. Because the materials are utilized to either trap the marker or increase the signal linked with detection, trace detection sensors have shown tremendous potential for the detection of chemical markers of EPs using NMs-based biosensors. These capabilities are hard to be realized by using conventional materials (Choi et al., 2020; Coroş et al., 2020; Crapnell et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2020). In all types of sensors, the identification component and physical transducer (electrode) are present, and the interactions between the electrode surface and the analytes have a substantial impact on sensing performance. The compact size and wide surface area of the sensing electrodes may result in quick reactions and great sensitivity to the targeted species from a sensory standpoint. Furthermore, interface and form changes to diverse electrode materials result in unique binding capabilities, allowing for highly selective, long-lasting, and precise pollution detection. The increased enthusiasm in NM-based biosensors has sparked a flurry of activity and research that could lead to next-generation innovations for environmental applications aimed at improving the sensing of EPs. For instance, Zhang et al. (2014) examined the recent developments and emerging themes in NMs-based biosensors for biological and environmental detection of organophosphate insecticides and nerve additives. In his article, the basic information, as well as a summary of immunoassay and electrochemical detection technologies were presented. According to the researchers, NMs based biosensors could be utilized as signal transducers or recognition agents to regulate current flow. For this purpose, electroactive tags were used to indicate the existence of analytes. The biosensor development team focused on improving the sensor efficiency for a variety of chemicals found in the ecosystem (like water, air and food) as well as the biological matrix (such as blood, urine and saliva) that were consistently monitored. They ended up to the conclusion that these NMs based biosensors exhibit precision, efficiency, sensitivity, selection, reproducibility, and stability which are all important characteristics to consider while detecting and analyzing the EPs in any specific sample. Similarly, Maheshwaran et al. (2021) developed a copper sulfide (CuS) nano-globules enhanced biosensor for potent-efficiency electrochemical detection of hazardous hydroquinone (HQE), a pharmaceutical waste. Herein, CuS NMs were synthesized using a hydrothermal technique, and their crystal framework, geometry, composition, and topographical were all thoroughly investigated. For HQE sensing, the CuS/GCE (GCE denotes glassy carbon electrode) produced a broad linear spectrum of 1–2287 mM, as well as enhanced LODs (0.135 mM). As a result, the CuS nano-globules-based electrochemical sensor could be strongly recommended for real-time HQE examination and could be further utilized as an excellent contender for the detection of several other EPs.

Taking into account, this review article presents a critical examination of the crucial role of NMs based biosensors in the detection and sensing of various pollutants (commonly known as emerging pollutants (EPs)) such as fertilizers, pesticides, dyes, pharmaceuticals waste, heavy metals, etc. An emphasis on the various forms of NMs based biosensors in EP's detection along with its detection mechanism and future perspectives have been discussed deeply in this review article.

Owing to its superior physicochemical attributes, high adsorption, high surface-to-volume ratio, reactive capacity and several other advantageous characteristics, NMs have emerged as potent introspective probes for not only improving specificity and sensitivity and also providing a riser's transformation to interpret the specific molecule domain (Awad et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019; Sohail et al., 2019; Thakkar et al., 2019). NMs with unique properties and functions pave the way for the introduction of novel analysis techniques that might be activated quickly in response to altering and existing chemical pollutants, EPs along with real-time ecological monitoring in soil, air, water, and meal safety, all of which are important for protecting public health and the environment such as nanoelectronics, nano sensors, nanotheranostics, enzyme immobilization etc. Because NMs and related nanocomposites have so many diverse physical, chemical, biological and sensing properties, a great deal of effort has gone into developing synthetic procedures with highly changeable shape, size, surface charge, and physicochemical properties in recent decades. The adsorption of nanomaterials with chemical or biomolecules, as well as their interaction with them, are crucial in the manufacturing of nanomaterials for catalysis and sensing applications. Although NMs serve a variety of roles in different electrochemical sensors, utilizing an NMs based biosensors offers numerous benefits:
•
effective catalytic property;
•
rapid mass transportation;
•
a relatively large sensor surface area; and
•
a well-regulated electrode microenvironment

The enhanced surface area to volume ratio of nanomaterials enables improved catalytic and sensing responsiveness due to the fast transit of analytes through NPs-based electrodes or sensors. The superior optical, magnetic, electrical, and catalytic capabilities indicate a significant advantage over macroscale materials, exhibiting outstanding specialized characteristics. NMs, in general, have a considerable proportion of surface atoms, high surface energy, temporal constriction, and fewer imperfections, all of which are linked to numerous sources (Cui et al., 2019; Kantize et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019; Renganathan et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019). For instance, Punnoose et al. (2021) focused on the environmentally friendly production of unmodified silver (Ag) NMs utilizing leaf extract (aqueous) from Mimosa diplotricha (MD) (AgNP-MD). The high selectivity of AgNP-MD in electronic and optoelectronic detection of the Hg2+ ion (5–45 μM) was demonstrated in this study. After efficient interaction of metal NMs with Hg2+ ion, the color transition of AgNP-MD to colorless, which correlates to a decrease in nanoparticle surface plasmon resonance (SPR), was promptly identified as optical sensing. A platinum (Pt) electrode modified with AgNP-MD was used for electrochemical voltammetric sensing (AgNP-MD-Pt). By utilizing differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis, the produced green sensor's limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ ion was revealed to be 1.46 M. Using real water samples, the relevance of unmodified AgNP-MD against Hg2+ ions was evaluated. Green AgNP-catalytic MD's potential in the removal of methylene blue and methyl orange was also examined in which degradations found to be quick, taking only a few mins, and had pseudo-first-order kinetics with significant linear regression.

Enhancing detecting interfaces with innovative NMs to produce faster electron transfer of biomolecules and greater selectivity has piqued attention in recent years. This benefit has sparked an investigation into the use of NMs-based biosensors in conjunction with biomolecules. These biosensors are being utilized in several applications, including food standards, clinical assessment, ecological management, and EPs detection. NMs in biosensors have numerous advantages, such as the ability to reduce overpotentials in several more analytically essential electrochemical reactions, confirm the reproducibility of some redox reactions that are irreversible at unchanged electrodes, and provide innovative labeling prospects, like multidetector abilities. Taking into account, several NMs-based biosensor systems have indeed been documented in academia, incorporating them into real-world in-situ technologies to monitor a wide range of ecological parameters, along with measuring trace heavy metals, monitoring water quality parameters (pH, dissolved oxygen, conductivity), and assessing carcinogenic and organic pollutants (N-nitroso compounds, aromatic amines and phenols). While using NMs as sensing elements, the existence of noble-metal NPs with acute ends or edges could be crucial. Du et al. (2021) reviewed the core concept of NMs based biosensors and their foremost sensing mechanisms, as well as the current advancement of common biosensors detection techniques for herbicide detection, through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV). Similarly, Maheshwaran et al. (2021) utilized a hydrothermal technique to synthesize CuS nano-globules. The major physical features of the nanomaterial, like morphology, crystal architecture, topography, and structure, all were examined. The analyte hydroquinone (HQE) was employed to illustrate the electrochemical detection capabilities of CuS nano-globules enhanced electrodes. The CuS-based sensor has a linear range of 1–2287 mM and a LOD of 0.135 mM after electrochemical testing. Furthermore, the CuS nano-globules-equipped electrode has a significant sensing capability towards the HQE, with excellent repeatability and durability. These findings clearly show that CuS nano-globules have exceptional electrochemical characteristics, and they are highly suggested for real-time analysis.

Similarly, in a research, Huang et al. (2019) discussed latest trends in electrochemical detection of a persistent toxic substance (PTS) utilizing metal oxide NPs and their composite NMs from the concept, processing, and application perspectives during the last few years as shown in Fig. S1 (Pérez-Fernández et al., 2020). The review was useful in understanding the advancement of electrochemical detection and in developing new NMs with superior electrochemical capability and excellent sensing and detection of EPs.

Furthermore, Tajik et al. (2021b) presented a paper on modern improvements in polymer nanocomposite-based biosensors for sensing contaminants in the environment. In situ polymerization, the simultaneous blending of nanofillers and polymer (solution-mixing and melt-mixing) and electrochemical methods were the most important approaches for polymer nanocomposites synthesis. The current application of polymer nanocomposites for electrochemical sensor fabrication as a novel strategy for monitoring and detecting environmental contaminants such as pesticides, hydrazine, phenolic compounds, heavy metal ions, nitrite and nitroaromatic compounds in diverse environments was summarised. Down below, Table 1 deliberates the usage of numerous sorts of NMs based biosensors utilized for the detection and remediation of EPs. For this purpose, several characterizations and detection techniques have been used such as UV, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), DPV, CV.

While investigating the literature, we observed that the use of NMs based biosensors as sensing components for environmental scanning has not previously been studied, thus the goal of this review is to thoroughly examine and highlight the significance of NMs based biosensors in the EPs successive detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials (NMs) based biosensors for EP's detection

ce:section.ce:para: The optical phenomena of fluorescence comprise labeling for the sensing analyte or molecules. The development of fluorescence-based biosensors has gotten a lot of interest as a result of several attributes such as excellent selectivity, specificity, and fast reaction time, enabling it to be used in medical diagnostics, environmental, and food quality monitoring activities. In this biosensor, long-range multipole interactions transmit nonradiative energy from an excited donor molecule (D) to the acceptor molecule (A) at the ground state in the incompletion phase. Fluorescence-based biosensors are frequently used in the detection of EPs because they could identify variations in angstroms to nanometers.

In a research, Fu et al. (2022) developed an innovative fluorescence biosensor comprised of magnetic beads, gold (Au) NPs, and DNA-stabilized silver nanoclusters to monitor Salmonella Typhimurium, a worldwide prevalent foodborne bacterium that could cause foodborne infectious illness epidemics. A fluorescence quenching device having a “sandwich structure” was created by combining magnetic beads, AuNPs and aptamer. The aptamer served as a link, releasing AuNPs through the system due to its particular adherence to S. Typhimurium. Simultaneously, DNA-AgNCs (fluorescent DNA-stabilized silver nanoclusters) were produced. S. Typhimurium was detected using fluorescence intensity fluctuations generated by fluorescence resonance energy transit across AuNPs and DNA-AgNCs. Given a linear reaction to S. Typhimurium varying from 3.7 × 10−2 to 3.7 × 10−5 cfu/mL, the intended biosensor demonstrated excellent specificity and sensitivity. Within 2 h and 10 min, the LOD was determined to be 98 cfu/mL. Furthermore, with a LOD of 3.4 × 10−2 cfu/mL, this approach showed great utility for the tracing of S. Typhimurium in contaminated milk. As a result, it is possible to conclude that the newly designed NMs based fluorescent aptasensor has a lot of prospective for detecting S. Typhimurium in meals.

Similarly, For the accurate detection of dibutyl phthalate (DBP), Meng et al. (2022) developed a unique ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay centered on AgNPs. AgNPs were labeled on the secondary antibody (AgNPs@Ab2) in the detecting system for signal enhancement, with the goal of modulating hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) concentrations. Scopoletin (SC) fluorescence was successfully reduced by the H2O2 provided, but Amplex Red (AR) fluorescence was formed while AgNPs-Ab2 and antigen–primary antibody (Ab1) were associated by exact identification. The determined LOD, 90% inhibition under ideal conditions, was 0.86 ng/mL, with a linear range of 2.31–66.84 ng/mL, which was almost eleven times smaller than the horseradish peroxide (HRP)-based classical enzyme-linked immunoabsorbent assay (ELISA) with the same antibody. Meanwhile, combining dual-output ratiometric fluorescence assays with ELISA improved the inherent built-in rectification to the environment, as well as precision and accuracy (recoveries, 87.20–106.62%; CV, 2.57–6.54%), implying that it could be used to assess DBP levels in water samples.

Additionally, Kalkal et al. (2020) described how to make a fluorescence turn-on biosensor with biofunctionalized graphene (G) QDs as the energy supplier and AuNPs as the energy receiver for ultrasensitive monitoring of a marginal cell lung cancer biomarker. Material Studio software was used to improve the anticipated chemical structure of produced amine-N-GQDs, as well as a detailed analysis of empirical results to validate the existence of possible N-doping and amine functionalization spots. Antibodies against monoclonal neuron-specific enolase (anti-NSE) were covalently immobilized to amine-N-GQDs to form biofunctionalized GQDs (anti-NSE/amine-N-GQDs). A label-free and effective fluorescent biosensor centered on nanosurface energy transfer (NSET) involving anti-NSE/amine-N-GQDs and AuNPs was developed for NSE monitoring. Anti-NSE/amine-N-GQDs@AuNPs nanoprobe fluorescence response studies as a result of NSE antigen demonstrated a short reaction period (16 min), a wide LOD (0.1 ng/mL to 1000 ng/mL), and a moderate LODs (0.09 ng/mL). Furthermore, with a mean recovery rate of 94.69% in real specimens, the fluorescent biosensor functioned wonderfully.

Similarly, Gao et al. (2020) presented a fluorescence sensor array built on sulfonated conjugated microporous polymers (S-CMP) which were employed for high-throughput detection of numerous organic/inorganic pollutants. A two-step technique was used to make this S-CMP, which included a palladium (Pd)-catalyzed coupling reaction and a sulfonation reaction. S-CMP was capable of distinguishing twelve metal ions in an aqueous solution using cross-reactivity discrimination testing, indicating that the sensor array might be used as a “chemical nose.” Furthermore, when S-CMP was subjected to typical organic solvents of varying polarity, it exhibited notable changes in fluorescence intensities. S-CMP was used to effectively distinguish eleven organic small compounds using a dual-readout orthogonal identification approach. This S-CMP showed tremendous potential as a fluorescence sensor for environmental pollution assessment due to its benefits of simple construction and good selectivity.

Ge et al. (2019) created boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) as fluorescent probes for the production of ferric ion (Fe3+ ion) or phosphate sensors, along with a biosensor for cytochrome C. (Cyt C). A hydrothermal approach involving a one-step bottom-up molecular fusion of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and borax in sodium hydroxide was used to produce the B-GQDs. The synthesis was done in a large capacity autoclave (500 mL) yielding 71%, proving that B-GQDs can be made on a gram-scale. The B-GQDs had a single or bilayer graphene structure, excellent crystalline structure, homogeneous size, luminous (absolute photo-luminescence quantum yield of 16.8%), and excitation-independent green fluorescence as they were generated. Effective doping of B atoms in the lattice of GQDs permitted significant selectivity favoring Fe3+. Relying on the quenching of fluorescence and monitoring of B-GQDs by Fe3+ (LOD of 31.2 nM) and Fe3+-rich Cyt C (LOD of 5.9 g/mL) was demonstrated (turn-off model). Because it restored Fe3+-quenched fluorescence of B-GQDs, phosphate was monitored indirectly fluorescently with a LOD of 340 nM (turn-off-on model). Fe3+, Cyt C, and phosphate were also discovered in real specimens.

For the first time, Wu et al. (2019) produced three innovative bisbenzimidazole derivatives and used them as dual-functional fluorescence sensors to detect Ag+ and Fe3+ ions in a semi-aqueous environment with unique spectrum responses. After the inclusion of Ag+, the absorption intensity was dramatically reduced. The inclusion of Fe3+, on the other hand, resulted in a significant rise. At low concentrations of both Ag+ and Fe3+, there was a good linear relation, providing a quantifiable mechanism for their detection. The sensors also showed a differential fluorescence response to Ag+ and Fe3+, as well as a distinctive fluorescence color shift when exposed to UV radiation. Furthermore, no substantial alterations or interactions with other metal ions were identified. The N atom in the CN of the benzimidazole ring of sensor 4a bonded with Ag+ or Fe3+ ion to produce a metal complex, according to the sensing mechanism investigations. The 4-Ag+ complex system had only a static quenching mechanism, whereas the 4-Fe3+ complex system had both dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. Furthermore, sensors 4 performed consistently in solution over a pH range of 4 to 13 and responded quickly to Ag+ and Fe3+ with a response duration of 10 s.

Additionally, Kiruba Daniel et al. (2019) proposed a revolutionary “Turn-On” fluorescence sensor that relied on aptamer-templated zinc oxide (ZnO)-QDs that was later evolved into an electrical detecting system and used to detect Hg2+ quickly. When Hg2+ attached to the aptamer, it generated a T-Hg2+-T pair, which serves as a framework for generating ZnO-QDs. They noticed that when the concentration of Hg2+ increased, so did the creation of duplexes, which resulted in an increase in fluorescence. The device's LODs was 0.1 ppb (0.5 nM), and quantitative investigation revealed a linear detection range of 0.1 to 10,000 ppb. Electron microscopy was used to analyze the crystalline structure of the aptamer-templated ZnO-QDs in the presence of Hg2+. Subsequently, the sensing was turned into a basic electronic detection device prototype that worked. For the device's creation, an Arduino Nano board was attached to a photodiode and a light-emitting diode (LED) output. This was the first work to show that increasing the concentration of Hg2+, increased the fluorescence of aptamer-templated ZnO-QDs. This was also the first time an analyte has been added to the QD manufacturing process, resulting in fluorescence-based analyte sensing. As a result, a new fluorescence sensor centered on aptamer-templated ZnO-QDs was developed, with high specificity and sensitivity, and a simple electronic detecting method.

By post-grafting two forms of tetraphenylethene derivatives on mesoporous components, Wang et al. (2018a) created aggregation-induced emission luminogens (AIEgens)-functionalized mesoporous silica nanoparticles. The fluorescent chemical sensors FMSN-1 and FMSN-2 produced a high blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light. They both demonstrated quick and sensitive reactions to 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (2,4,6-TNP) using the fluorescence quenching method. The antibiotics furazolidone and nitrofurazone were also detected using these biosensors, with LOD of 3.8 μM and 7.2 μM, correspondingly. The photo-induced energy transfer quenching mechanism may be responsible for the high degree quenching efficiency for analytes. The findings showed that AIEgens-functionalized mesoporous silica materials are attractive prospects for use as fluorescent chemosensors in water systems for detecting nitroaromatic explosives and antibiotics.

By mounting a naphthalimide-based fluorescent probe onto the surface of mesoporous silica nanoparticles, Wu et al. (2018) developed a unique bifunctional fluorescence sensor for simultaneous identification and eradication of Ag+ in aqueous solutions. In water specimens, the fluorescence sensor demonstrated excellent sensitivity for Ag+ and a 2.5-fold increase in fluorescence intensity when Ag+ was added. Furthermore, the fluorescence sensor demonstrated excellent linearity at low Ag+ concentrations, with LODs of 7.2 × 10−6 μM. The fluorescence sensor was also used as an adsorbent to remove Ag+ from water samples, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 14.8 mg/g. By altering the pH of the aqueous phase, the fluorescence sensor was easily regenerated and recovered at least eight times. All of these findings suggested that a fluorescence sensor could be used to detect and remove Ag+ from aqueous samples.

Liu et al. (2018) created a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the exceedingly precise and sensitive monitoring of 4-nitrophenol using a unique molecularly imprinted core-shell nanohybrid. This sensor was powered by the transmission of fluorescence resonance energy through photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) and 4-nitrophenol. To develop this sensor, organosilane-functionalized CDs were coupled to silica-coated CdSe quantum dots (CdSe@SiO2) via SiO linkages. The nanohybrids were further improved by embedding a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coating over the ratiometric fluorescence sensor using a simple sol-gel polymerization technique. The structure, chemical properties, and optical properties of the molecularly imprinted dual-emission fluorescent probe were investigated using TEM. They examined how the fluorescence pattern of CdSe@SiO2/CD/MIP changed when different amounts of 4-nitrophenol were added. The fluorescence intensity at 455 nm was very sensitive to the 4-nitrophenol concentration, as shown in Fig. 2a, and decreased as the 4-nitrophenol concentration increased. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the CdSe-QDs at 615 nm showed no noticeable variation, showing that CdSe-QDs encased in silicon dioxide (SiO2) are suitable to be used as a reference signal. The plot of Fb/Fr against 4-nitrophenol concentrations ranging from 0.051 to 13.7 g/mL, displayed significant linearity and a high correlation value (R2 = 0.985), as shown in Fig. 2b. The fluorescence intensities of the CdSe@SiO2/CD/MIP nano-hybrid at 455 and 615 nm, respectively, were indicated by Fb and Fr. The LODs was predicted to be 0.026 ng/mL (3/k) based on these findings, which was substantially lower than the US EPA's approved limit in drinking water (i.e., 60 ng/L), indicating that the molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence biosensor has practical application potential.

Beneath, Table 2 includes the several NMs based fluorescent biosensor encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — Simple operation, quick reaction, numerous analyses, high sensitivity, and strong selectivity.
Disadvantages — not all compounds fluoresce.

Electrochemical biosensors are perhaps the most extensively explored and utilized biosensors, with their working assumptions based on the analyte's and transducer's electrochemical properties. Electrochemical biosensors have a remarkable level of sensibility, specificity, and detection capabilities. An electrochemical process involving the bioreceptor and the analyte happens on the transducer surface, generating observable electrochemical signs in measures of current, voltage, impedance, and capacitance. Fig. S2 shows schematic schematics for biosensors that are (a) amperometric/voltammetric, (b) potentiometric, (c) conductometric, and (d) impedimetric (showing the analogous circuit) (Naresh and Lee, 2021).

High sensitivity readings and simultaneous detection of numerous analytes are two advantages of these sensors. Taking into consideration, Li et al. (2022) devised a disposable electrochemical biosensor for the precise and quick monitoring and detection of NSE at the point of care (POC). The substratum for trapping Ab1 and the signal probe for labeling Ab2 were iron (II, III) oxide (Fe3O4) and CuS nanoparticles, respectively. For convenient sample segregation, the disposable syringe filter was included in the assessment technique, allowing for no-wash detection. The large-sized immunocomplex of Fe3O4Ab1/NSE/CuSAb2 was prevented on the membrane because the syringe filters with 200 nm pore diameter solely permitted the small nanoparticles of CuSAb2 to penetrate. The CuSAb2 particles were driven out of the syringe, where they conducted electron transfer among Cu2+ and Cu+, resulting in a current signal recorded by the Au electrode. Under optimal conditions, the no-washing biosensor demonstrated a wide linear concentration scope (50 ng/mL–100 ng/mL) exhibiting LOD of 33 ng/mL (S/N = 3). The biosensor also demonstrated high selectivity, preservation durability, and repeatability. The no-washing biosensor's remarkable characteristics render it more appropriate for POC testing.

Campaña et al. (2019) presented a review of recent advances in NMs-based electrochemical biosensors, including modeling and multiphysics simulation, designing, manufacturing, validation, and operating approaches. Electrochemical biosensors detect the electric potential difference or current applying to the specimen electric potential. The analyte-enzyme process was carried out in this case at a specified potential range where current changes can be observed. Amperometric sensors are a type of voltammetric sensor in which the potential is maintained at a constant value. The conductometric sensors are the second category, and they measure variations in the conductance of the specimen medium throughout redox reactions. The third type is potentiometric sensors, which use two non-polarizable electrodes to monitor potential differences (working and reference electrodes). Optical biosensors detect variations in the spectroscopic characteristics of enzyme catalysis products like fluorescence and/or absorption. UV-VIS, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy are some of the spectroscopic techniques utilized. Furthermore, thermal biosensors monitor enthalpy variations caused by reactions on the electrode surface electrons created by analyte oxidation during catalysis. As a consequence, the amount of molecular oxygen in the medium is reduced, resulting in the formation of water molecules. This sort of biosensor could also be characterized by the electrical magnitude at which the potential difference is detected, relying on the detecting technology utilized in the electrode. Voltammetric sensors are the first category, and they detect the current flow across an electrode as a reaction to an induced electric potential. The analyte-enzyme reaction is carried out in this case at a specified potential range where current changes can be observed. Amperometric sensors are a type of voltammetric sensor in which the potential is maintained at a constant value. The conductometric sensors are the second category, and they measure variations in the conductance of the specimen medium throughout redox reactions. The third type is potentiometric sensors, which use two non-polarizable electrodes to measure potential differences; the two electrodes are referred to as the working and reference electrodes.

In research, Nodehi et al. (2021) used an electrodeposited nanoporous Au electrode to create a simple and robust electrochemical biosensor. A layering of AuNPs applied on the surface of SBA-15-Met's three-dimensional (3D) mesoporous structures offered a pristine, durable, and biocompatible surface for chain bisphenol (A) (BPA) aptamers to self-assemble, resulting in significant improvements in molecule detection and signal amplification. A multivariate experimental approach was used to optimize the most critical aspects influencing the architecture of the aptasensor, like aptamer concentration, BPA incubation duration, and solution pH. A five-level central composite structure and response surface methodology were employed for this. The reported biosensor showed a wide linear scope of 10 to 1200 pM under optimal conditions, featuring an ultralow LOD of 3.65 nM for BPA. As per molecular docking calculations, several hydrogen bonds and π-π interactions resulted in a strong affinity among the aptamer and BPA molecules, resulting in high selectivity and specificity for BPA detection in food samples for the proposed biosensor.

Negrea et al. (2021) exhibited an innovative electrochemical sensor for detecting tetracycline (TC) in aqueous solution by amending a commercial boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode with graphene oxide (GO) reduced electrochemically and then further electrodecorated using Ag. The Ag/GO/BDD electrode was selected from a series of BDD, GO electroreduced over BDD (GO/BDD), and silver electrodeposited onto BDD (modulation amplitude of 200 mV, Square-wave voltammetry (SWV) performed at a step potential of 5 mV, and frequency of 10 Hz in alkaline medium produced the optimum findings in terms of lowest LOD of 5 nM. Because of the inclusion of chloride, which interacts with TC detection, the alkaline supportive electrolyte-based approach was restricted for water monitoring; although, it could be used for quantitative analysis of pharmaceutical formulations. The addition of 0.1 M sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) as a supporting electrolyte removed chloride interference and allowed Ag/GO/BDD to be utilized in the realistic determination of TC in water.

To detect thiourea as a contaminant, Jodan et al. (2020) used an electrochemical sensor focused on a GCE augmented using AgNPs and alizarin yellow. In this study, alizarin yellow polymer and AgNPs were used to modify GCE using electro-polymerization and chronoamperometry, accordingly. SEM and EDX analysis were used to establish the properties of the redesigned electrode, and thiourea concentration was determined using CV. The linear range, LOD, and sensitivity were determined to be 10 to 940 nM, 3.3 μM, and 0.181 nM, correspondingly. On the efficacy of the sensor in identifying thiourea, the durability and repeatability of the sensor response, and the interruption impact of certain anion species, were explored. The findings showed that the sensor had the best reliability and repeatability and that the examined species had no influence on the sensor's capability to detect thiourea.

For precise detection of amoxicillin, Wong et al. (2020) developed a unique electrochemical biosensor centered on a melange of nanomaterials like Printex 6 L Carbon and cadmium telluride QDs within a poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate film. The combination of these materials increased electrochemical properties, electrode surface area and electron transfer rate, leading to a large amplitude of anodic spike current for amoxicillin detection. The antibiotic's electrochemical composition was determined using SWV. The suggested sensor demonstrated great sensitivity, reproducibility, and reliability to amoxicillin detection under optimum experimental circumstances, with an analytical curve in the amoxicillin concentration range of 0.90 to 69 mol L−1, and a low LOD of 50 nmol L−1. Potential biological interferences and commonly used medications like uric acid, paracetamol and caffeine did not affect the electrochemical output of amoxicillin. The sensor was demonstrated to be a viable alternative for not only the evaluation of pharmaceuticals (commercial tablets) and clinical specimens (urine) but also the testing of food quality without the need for any specimen pre-treatment and the use of a simple measurement apparatus.

Soares et al. (2020) demonstrated the creation of an ultrasensitive, label-free laser-induced graphene (LIG) electrode functionalized with antibodies for electrochemical detection of Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium, a food-borne disease. In this study, the LIG electrodes were created by laser induction on a polyimide film in ambient conditions, removing the need for high-temperature, vacuum environments, and metal seed catalysts, which were commonly associated with graphene-based electrodes formed through chemical vapor accumulation methods. The LIG biosensors were capable to identify live Salmonella in chicken broth over a large linear range (25 to 105 CFU/mL) and LOD (13 CFU/mL; n = 3, mean standard deviation) after being functionalized with Salmonella antibodies. Despite the use of specimen preconcentration or redox labeling procedures, these findings were obtained with an average reaction time of 22 min. Furthermore, these LIG immunosensors were very selective, as evidenced by their lack of sensitivity to other bacteria strains. Such findings demonstrated that LIG-based electrodes might be used for electrochemical immunosensing in principle as a viable option for fast and low-cost pathogen screening in food processing plants prior to infected commodities reaching the customer.

Tian et al. (2018) employed a GCE to design CuO-TiO2 hybrid nanocomposites as a nonenzymatic electrochemical biosensor for the highly selective and sensitive identification of methyl parathion. CV was utilized to analyze the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrode, revealing that it may be utilized to detect methyl parathion. Under optimized experimental parameters, DPV was utilized to assess methyl parathion detection capability, and it was discovered that the altered electrode could indeed detect methyl parathion sensitively over a wide dynamic detection range of 0 ppb to 2000 ppb, with a lower LOD of 1.21 ppb. Other interfering compounds revealed no effect on the detection of methyl parathion. In particular, the modified electrode was employed to detect methyl parathion in real ground water specimens and showed effective sensing properties. The electrochemical sensor developed has a promising possibility for detecting methyl parathion and has opened up new avenues for detecting additional organophosphorous pesticides in ground water. Beneath, Table 3 includes the several NMs based electrochemical biosensor encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — Resilience, small size, and high LODs, even with small analyte quantities.
Disadvantages — Narrow or limited temperature range, short or limited shelf life, cross-sensitivity of other gases and the greater the exposure to the target gas, the shorter the life span.

QDs are inorganic nanocrystals (NCs) that correspond to the 0D NMs group and exhibit distinctive optical features such as broad excitation, limited size-tunable emission spectra, strong photochemical resilience, and low light absorption. They have been effectively used during the development of optical biosensors to recognize pharmaceutical analytes, organic substances, and biomolecules like nucleic acids, enzymes, proteins, amino acids, carbohydrates, and neurotransmitters, mostly as substitutes to fluorophores. They have also been used to identify cancer in live animals. Owing to their high specificity, ultra-high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, compact size, quick analyte detection and size-dependent emission wavelength, they are suitable prospects for multiplexed optical bioanalysis. These QDs when integrate with nanomaterials for the manufacturing of a potent biosensor can enhance the sensing potency of the biosensors. In a research, Mao et al. (2022) developed a dual-readout lateral flow immunoassay centered on polydopamine-coated AuNPs for the specific and sensitive detection of carbendazim (CBD) in agriproducts. The oxidation of dopamine was used to bind colloidal AuNPs using polydopamines (PDA) to generate Au@PDA nanoparticles in this research. The Au@PDA nanoparticles facilitated fluorescence quenching and recovery in ZnCdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs), culminating in a reverse fluorescence amplification detection method for CBD. CBD detection was achieved via a competition between CBD and the immobilized antigen for Au@PDAs labeled antibody attachment, which resulted in a significant fluorescence amplification and colorimetry drop that was connected to the CBD concentration. The lateral flow immune assay (LFIA) provided dual output modalities, which used the colorimetry signal in natural light and the fluorescence signal in UV light. In the colorimetric and fluorometric signal modalities, the cut-off values for CBD detection were 0.5 g/mL and 0.0156 g/mL, respectively. The LFIA sensitivity of the fluorescence mode was 32 times higher than that of the colorimetry mode. When thiabendazole, benomyl, thiophanate-methyl, and thiophanate-ethyl were determined by LFIA, there was very little cross reactivity. The LFIA system relied heavily on the optical efficiency and robustness of detecting probes (Au@PDAs-mAb and QDs-OVA). In the LFIA platform, Au@PDAs-mAb was trapped by CBD-OVA at the T line, which resulted in trapped Au@PDAs-mAb quenching the fluorescence of ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs owing to a FRET from ZnCdSe/ZnS-QDs to the trapped Au@PDAs-mAb (Fig. 3 ). An overlap between the donor's emission spectra and the acceptor's absorption spectrum was necessary for FRET-based quenching. PDA attached to the nanoparticles surface resulted in greater UV–vis absorption than bare nanoparticles in the colorimetry mode of the LFIA, resulting in a darker color. A thin PDA layer also increased the absorbance of Au@PDAs, but a thick PDA coating caused Au@PDAs to aggregate, resulting in a bigger absorption peak and lower absorbance.

Additionally, Zhang et al. (2022) generated surface molecularly imprinted layer coated CdSe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots (MIP-CdSe/CdS/ZnS-QDs), which were used as specific optosensing substances in fluorescence nanosensors for diethylstilbestrol (DES) and cypermethrin (CYP), correspondingly. The fluorescence quenching sensitivity of MIP-CdSe/CdS/ZnS-QDs to DES/CYP was demonstrated using functional group, surface morphology and fluorescence spectroscopy. In the spectrum of 0.050–20.0 mg/L and 0.10–20.0 mg/L, strong linear correlations for DES and CYP were detected under their respective ideal circumstances. Furthermore, for the seawater and fish specimens, the recoveries were 91.4%–101.5% with an RSD value of less than 5.4%. The LODs in seawater and fish could be as low as 0.004 g/L and 3.0 g/kg (DES) and as high as 0.3 g/L and 5.0 g/kg (CYP). This research proposed a technique for producing an effective fluorescence nanosensor using a dual-template MIP-CdSe/CdS/ZnS-QDs to monitor distinct analytes at their respective optimal circumstances. Furthermore, the created fluorescent nanosensor was effectively used to identify DES or CYP in fish and seawater samples offering high precision, specificity, and reliability under their respective ideal circumstances. As a result, the proposed MIP-QDs showed promise for DES/CYP adsorption removal and evaluation in real specimens.

Wang et al. (2021) developed GO-QDs centered nanotree demonstrated the detection of Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). AFB1is perhaps the most poisonous metabolite of mycotoxins, contaminating food crops on a regular basis and posing a major health risk to humans. As a result, it is critical to investigate a methodology for detecting AFB1 that is both quick and sensitive. A fluorescence aptasensor for detecting AFB1 was presented consisting on a DNA walker, DNA tetrahedron nanostructures (DTNs), and network hybridization chain reaction (HCR). The DNA walker arrangement was used as a signal amplifier triggered by the AFB1 target, where the DNA walker chain walked on a 3D track of AuNPs and joined complementary sequences of DTNs, causing endonuclease to cut and release a large number of DTNs. Four single-stranded DNA molecules with multiple fulcrums were used to create DTNs. DTNs increased the assembly rate of GO-QDs based nanotrees by providing different reaction orientations for HCR. Target AFB1 bonded with aptamers in the context of target AFB1 and activate secondary signal amplification to attain sensitive detection. This aptasensor had a LOD of 0.492 μg/mL and detected AFB1 in a linear range of 11,000 μg/mL. Furthermore, this approach could be used to detect additional harmful compounds.

Similarly, Cui et al. (2021) was able to effectively build a QDs immunofluorescence biosensor for the detection of S. aureus. This biosensor combined biosensor characteristics with the increased sensibility of antigen-antibody immunological interactions, as well as the sensitivity and persistence of QDs fluorescence. Due to the excitation wavelength of QDs was 565 nm, the excitation light source was a semiconductor laser having a modulation frequency of 1 kHz and a wavelength of 532 nm. Through its pigtail fiber, the laser was connected to one of the inputs of an optical fiber coupler (2 2, couple ratio: 20:80). The fluorescence optical fiber connector was used to attach the combined tapered fiber probe to the 20% end of the coupler. The input laser light was sent to the fiber probe to stimulate QDs that were attached to an antibody that had been caught on the surface of the fiber probe. The fiber probe also gathered the fluorescence signal of QDs with a wavelength of 605 nm and delivered it back to the fiber coupler's other input end. The fluorescence signal was screened using a long-pass filter featuring a cut-on wavelength of 600 nm to remove the excitation light (532 nm). The fluorescence signal was then transmitted to an avalanche photodiode, which was employed for photo-electronic conversion and detection in a data acquisition (DAQ) system. Digital signals from the DAQ system were subsequently analyzed with LabVIEW utilizing a correlation detection technique centered on a phase-locked filtering algorithm to improve signal resolution. Afterward, the computer screen exhibited the fluorescence signal.

The SEM scans of the optical fiber probes trapping S. aureus, as shown in Fig. S3, revealed that the higher the quantity of the S. aureus solution, the higher S. aureus collected by the probes (Cui et al., 2021). As the S. aureus suspension concentration expanded (from 1 × 10−4 to 1 × 10−8 CFU/mL), the trapping levels of the S. aureus strain on the surface of the probes progressively expanded in S. aureus solutions at varied concentrations. The biosensor had high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting S. aureus, according to the findings. The LODs for S. aureus were 1 × 10−4 or even 1 × 10−3 CFU/mL, and fluorescence intensity had a significant positive linear connection with the logarithm of S. aureus concentration in the spectrum of 103–107 CFU/mL (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9731, P = 0.011). A specificity test revealed that this biosensor might efficaciously differentiate S. aureus (1 × 10−4 CFU/mL and above) from other common pathogenic (non-S. aureus) bacteria found in nosocomial infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumonia, Acinetobacter baumannii, and E. coli. Furthermore, the entire detecting process took approximately 2 h.

Talari et al. (2021) constructed an optical apta-nanosensor centered on reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), which they utilized to detect diazinon, among the most extensively utilized organophosphorus pesticides. Because of the high fluorescence emission and optical stability of GQDs and rGQDs, effective optical transducers were built, and accurate detection techniques were developed using them. Utilizing rGQDs, a diazinon-specific aptamer, and MWCNTs, a simple and inexpensive fluorescence method was developed to trace and measure diazinon with a LOD of 0.4 nM (0.1 g/L) in the range of 4 to 31 nM, meeting the European Union and World Health Organization specifications and standards. The real applicability of the innovative apta-nanosensor was subsequently confirmed by detecting and classifying diazinon in real samples, like tap water, urine, river water, agricultural runoff water, to evaluate apta-nanosensor efficiency. The procured outcomes disclosed that, in the existence of other pesticides, the emission recoveries were kept intact due to the lack of binding affinity for the diazinon specific aptamer, and thus, the devised apta-nanosensor was allowed to identify diazinon pesticide with high precision and selectivity in real samples. As a result, the precise apta-nanosensor created in this study offered a sensitive, low-cost, quick, and portable technology for detecting diazinon over other pesticides in contaminated areas.

Hu et al. (2021) employed a green one-step approach to produce a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based biosensor for selective and specific detection of E. coli. A microwave-assisted approach was used to make CQDs from the orange peel as a carbon resource. Under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, the CQDs emitted a bright green fluorescence. CQDs labeled with aptamer (aptamer-CQDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) labeled with complementary DNA were used to create a fluorescent probe (CQDs-MNPs) for E. coli (cDNA-MNPs). With the inclusion of E. coli, the fluorescent intensity of the CQDs-MNPs was reduced. A fluorescent technique with a detection range of 500–106 CFU/mL and LOD of 487 CFU/mL was developed using the linearity between fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentration. This technique was used to analyze E. coli-contaminated milk samples, and the results were consistent with those attained by plate-counting methods. This luminous probe has a lot of promise in terms of ensuring food quality and safety. Beneath, Table 4 includes the several NMs based quantum dots (QDs) biosensors encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.

Advantages — Unique and superior optical and electronic properties, such as high brightness, good photostability, broad absorption spectrum, narrow and size-tunable emission spectrum, large stokes shift, versatile surface modification, and distinctive photoelectrochemical activity.
Disadvantages — Highly toxic and require external stimuli to be stable.

Carbon materials are essential in electrochemical research for a myriad of purposes. In theory, carbon materials are great electronic conductors as they are inexpensive, ubiquitous, easy to work with, chemical inertness, and excellent for manufacturing composite. Other materials may undergo electrochemical conversions or induce electrolyte decomposition, but carbon compounds can perform a variety of electrochemical activities (Abdelbasir et al., 2018; Jana et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2018). However, in electrochemistry, their chemical inertness is a challenge. Subsequent detection/quantification and electrocatalytic conversion require interaction between both the electrode component and the analyte. Various defect and functional groups in carbon compounds in the substance that are either naturally occurring or artificially created may help identify it. Additionally, insecticides with aromatic rings could interact with carbon via π-π stacking interactions, allowing for essential interactions within the sample prior to charge transfer. While carbon is often utilized as a basis for various metallic or oxide nanostructures or as a scaffolding for bioactive molecules, some research has focused on “all‑carbon” electrodes for the detection of pollutants including pesticides, fungicides, dyes, heavy metals using basic electrochemical properties. When metallic or oxide chemicals are utilized, more complicated electrochemical techniques are often used to improve the detection performance of carbon-based sensing devices. These systems are frequently highly robust and require minimal special maintenance due to the attractive properties of carbon materials. In a study, Ma et al. (2021) utilized conventional techniques to manufacture copper/cobalt/nitrogen-doped 3D hollow nanocarbon spheres (Cu/Co/N-HNCS) novel nanomaterial, and then utilized Cu/Co/N-HNCS in electrochemical sensing based biosensing using acetaminophen (ACAP), catechol (CC), and hydroquinone (HQ) as phenolic models. Three phenolics were electrochemically evaluated at an array of customized electrodes (Co/N-HNCS, Cu/N-HNCS, Cu/Co/N-HNCS and N-HNCS customized electrodes); in the meantime, ACAP was chosen as a typical goal to study the sensing capacities of Cu/Co/N-HNCS against phenolics in greater depth. Due to the synergistic qualities of each component in NMs, the Cu/Co/N-HNCS electrode exhibited significant sensing capacities, with a LOD for ACAP of 0.039 nM and a wide linear region, according to the findings. The presented ways of manufacturing tri-element doped carbon NMs and sensing phenolics are revealed to be convenient, minimal price, and ecologically friendly, purposing their utilization in a variety of situations.

Similarly, Su et al. (2021) focused on the developments and emerging patterns in pesticide monitoring (such as neonicotinoids, organophosphorus and carbamates pesticides) using fluorescence carbon NMs-based biosensors, with a focus on zero-dimensional (0D) CD-GQDs, 2D carbon nitride nanosheets, and 3D carbon-based metal/covalent organic frameworks (COF). In this study, several primary detection units, such as an antibody, enzyme, aptamer and MIP, were used to classify these fluorometric approaches, and they were found to have high efficacy for pesticides with a LOD of 1 μg/mL. Their team covered the extant obstacles to viable sensor layouts, as well as future views for advances in fluorescent carbon nanomaterials-based biosensors, in addition to comparing the efficiency of these novel sensors to other methodologies. Additionally, Torrinha et al. (2020) wrote a paper about using nanostructured carbon-based electrochemical (bio)sensors to detect EPs in rivers and aquatic creatures. According to the researchers, carbon-based NMs found to be arguably the least commonly utilized nanostructures in the manufacturing of (bio)sensors due to their simple and well-comprised manufacturing techniques, ready accessibility, cheap price, strong chemical stability, and minimal cytotoxicity. Furthermore, their high specific surface area, which also enables for adhesion and exceptional structural stability for biomolecules, as well as their reasonably good conductivity, that facilitates adequate electron transfer rates, have been pertinent and beneficial characteristics, rationalizing their vital role in electrochemical (bio)sensor advancement for the significant detection of the broad spectrum of EPs. The involvement of carbon nanomaterials (CNTs, carbon black, carbon nanofibers, carbon nanopowder, fullerene, graphene, biochar NPs), utilized specifically or in combination with the other NMs, to the domain of environment protection (bio)sensing, and, in particular, to the evaluation of EPs in the ecosystem.

Ye et al. (2020) suggested a precise FRET biosensor to identify the DNA oxidation damage product 8-hydroxy-2-deoxyguanosine (8-OHdG), which is secreted in human urine. The FRET biosensor used a nanoporous alumina membrane with CDs as fluorescence donors and a CDs-modified nanoporous alumina membrane. As signal quenchers, AuNPs were encased in zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (Au@ZIF-8 nanoparticles). 8-OHdG antibody was used to biofunctionalized CDs and Au@ZIF-8 NPs. The capture of 8-OHdG on membrane substrates could reduce the gap between Au@ZIF-8 NPs and CDs. The fluorescence of CDs was suppressed by Au@ZIF-8 NPs with 350 nm excitation, resulting in the FRET phenomenon. The efficacy of quenching was investigated finding the LOD to be 0.31 nM. The FRET biosensor's interference testing revealed that it had high selectivity for detecting 8-OHdG. The biosensor may detect urinary 8-OHdG accurately and specifically using simple sample preparation techniques. It was found to be effective in detecting DNA damage biomarkers in urine and related body fluids. The surface properties of nanoporous alumina membranes without and with 8-OHdG trapped on nanoporous alumina membranes are shown in Fig. S4 (Ye et al., 2020). A thin platinum coating was deposited on the specimen surface utilizing focused ion beam (FIB) driven deposition to increase conductivity for SEM observation. Following silanization and modification with CDs, honeycomb-like nanoporous membranes become coarse (Fig. S4a). Covalent bonding was used to attach CDs to nanoporous alumina membranes. A single CD was too narrow to be seen with a scanning electron microscope. Furthermore, a tweaked 8-OHdG antibody was able to capture 8-OHdG molecules on CDs. Au@ZIF-8 NPs were used to identify 8-OHdG molecules on nanoporous alumina membranes. As seen by the red arrows in Fig. S4b, minute agglomerated particles covered the surfaces of nanoporous alumina membranes. It was due to the conjugation of Au@ZIF-8 nanoparticles.

Zhang et al. (2019a) demonstrated how to synthesize Pd-NPs with consistently coated porous activated carbon (PAC) using an effective approach. Using a potassium hydroxide (KOH) activation and heat reduction technique, the Pd-NPs were reduced in-situ on PAC (Pd@PAC). According to the morphological analysis, Pd@PAC had typically interconnected micropores and mesopores structure, with Pd-NPs equally disseminated across the PAC surface. Square wave anodic stripping voltammetry was utilized to detect trace Cd2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ ions using the Pd@PAC modified GCE (Pd@PAC/GCE). It demonstrated great sensitivity and selectivity in the sequential and specific detection of heavy metal ions, excellent anti-interference, repeatability, repeatability, and stability under the same experimental settings.
