Furthermore, the Pd@PAC/GCE was used to detect heavy metal ions in real sample species, demonstrating that it might be used for real-world detection. Pd@PAC has remarkable electrochemical performance due to its porous architecture and Pd-NPs; its interlinked micropores and mesopores design enhance mass diffusion and promote the deposition-stripping mechanism of heavy metal ions. Pd-NPs enhanced the frequency of active sites and improved conductivity significantly. The current study proposed a green and practical way for fabricating effective electrochemical sensors and electrocatalytic devices. Table S1 includes the several carbon-based NMs biosensors encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — High surface-volume ratio, high electrical conductivity, chemical stability and strong mechanical strength.
Disadvantages — No standardization in production and difficulties due to the high agglomeration tendency during production

Graphene is a carbon compound formed up of a single sheet of carbon atoms that are arranged in a honeycomb pattern. The most common members of this family are graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The p orbitals of each carbon atom coincide with the p orbitals of three surrounding carbon atoms to produce a delocalized big p bond in graphene, a two-dimensional layered material comprised of a thin sheet of carbon atoms with sp2 hybrid bonds. Electron movement is unrestricted in this region. With its huge relative surface area, high thermal efficiency, high mechanical strength and excellent optical attributes, graphene is an appealing candidate for catalyst carriers and functional composite materials. GO has fewer carboxyl groups, epoxy groups, and other oxygen-containing functional groups than other carbon-based materials GO. Because of its high dispersibility and high catalytic strength, GO is extensively utilized in the field of electrochemical sensors. Mechanical peeling, chemical vapor deposition, and graphene oxide reduction are the most common methods for producing graphene. Graphene electrochemical applications have lately received increased interest because of their superior physico-chemical features, cheap fabrication procedures, and the context of functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxide, carboxyl (Alharbi et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2018; Rhouati et al., 2018). NMs on graphene not merely avoid aggregation but also provides excellent support for nanomaterials. Graphene-related nanocomposites have been successfully manufactured and used to detect various EPs such as pesticides and heavy metals. To synthesize GO supported by AgNPs, simultaneous reduction of Ag (I) on GO with cyclodextrin as a stabilizer and ascorbic acid as a reductant was used. AgNPs were equally dispersed over GO, according to TEM and SEM. With a sensitivity of 180.5 mA/mM and a LOD of 0.24 nM, the findings demonstrated that AgNPs/GO/GCE detection of As(III) exhibited a stronger electrochemical signal.

In a research, Olugbenga Osikoya and Poomani Govender (2021) developed a benzene-derived graphene-AuNPs-based biosensor for tetracycline (TC) detection using chronoamperometry. A compositional analysis using XRD and Raman spectroscopy confirmed the existence of few-layer graphene. According to SEM and AFM examinations, the synthesized graphene was a thin flat sheet-like substance with an immense surface area and a thickness of less than 1 nm. The improved electrode had a sensitivity of 1.86 × 10−2 A/mM/cm2 and responded promptly to tetracycline detection in the study. The linear contrast ratio ranged from 2.90 × 10−1 M to 1.53 × 10−3 M, with a theoretical LOD of 1.60 × 10−1 M (S/N = 3). The diffusion-controlled oxidation of TC was revealed by CV evaluation of the customized electrodes. Similarly, Ponomarenko and Wehrens (2007) created biosensors based on GQDs that have compact dimensions, elevated bio-compatibility, physico-chemical durability, and diffusion. It had a wide range of utilization due to its unique sp2 carbon atom and high p conjugated arrangement, covering energy, electrical/optical/chemical catalysis and biological assessment. GQDs demonstrated to be appropriate for EPs detection utilizing a number of transduction methods owing to their superior efficacy, nanoscale size, and varied target binding capabilities. The enzyme efficiently bridged the gap between the sensor element and the target analyte as a common recognition element, enabling pesticide concentration monitoring. Owing to the extraordinary optical properties of GQDs and the enzyme's catalytic abilities, some high-efficiency fluorescence approaches showed superior sensing efficacy when contrasted to typical semiconductor QDs.

Hashemi et al. (2022) developed a high-performance sensor centered on the integration of rGO and brominated white polyaniline (PANi) flakes for the rapid and precise monitoring of Cd (II) and Pb (II) in biological and non-biological material. With a LOD/quantification limit of 7.3 nM/85 nM and 6.5 nM/92 nM, correspondingly, the as-developed arrangement concurrently recognized both Pb (II) and Cd (II) in a differentiable way. The sensor also showed excellent sensitivity towards Pb (II) and Cd (II) of about 4547.77 and 3914.01 A/M.cm−2, correspondingly, as well as a significant recovery rate in wastewater samples and human blood plasma. The synthesized complex demonstrated to be an excellent foundation for detecting and recovering heavy metal ions in a variety of aquatic samples.

Pifferi et al. (2022) used a colloidal technique to develop a hybrid nanocomposite made of rGO sheets that were surface-functionalized via π-π interactions with 1-pyrene carboxylic acid (PCA) and decorated in situ with AuNPs. The hybrid nanocomposite was examined by CV and EIS in terms of the individual components, both raw and coated as a layer-by-layer connection onto the electrode. The findings showed that the hybrid nanocomposite had higher electrochemical activity than the individual constituents, emphasizing the importance of the electrode's nanostructured surface morphology and the PCA coupling agent at the NPs-rGO interphase in increasing nanocomposite electroactivity. The electrodes modified by PCA-rGO/AuNPs were investigated for electroanalytical detection of As(III) in water using a typical three-electrode cell and anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV). A preconcentration phase was employed to reduce As2O3 to As(0), followed by a linear anodic scan to promote reoxidation to As in the stripping process (III). The ideal accumulation potential in the cathodic area was discovered to be 0.8 V due to the simultaneous release of hydrogen at the electrodes. It was not able to efficiently deposit As(III) at the electrode surface via reduction at lower potentials. Fig. S5 shows the ASV scans obtained from a 10−5 M As2O3 aqueous medium at the PCA-rGO/AuNPs, AuNPs, PCA-rGO, and PCA-rGO@AuNPs electrodes at an accumulation period of 60 s and voltage of 0.8 V (Pifferi et al., 2022).

In the above-mentioned experiment, no oxidation currents were seen at the PCA-rGO electrode as the experiment progressed. As a result, the electrode was barely electroactive in the As(0) oxidation, emphasizing the significance of AuNPs in the reaction's generation. ASV scans also indicated two distinct oxidation peaks at roughly 0.2 V (SCE) and + 0.4 V (SCE), respectively (Fig. S5a). To interpret and assign the oxidation peaks, anodic scans within voltage regimes of 0.4 V (SCE) to +0.1 V (SCE) and + 0.1 V (SCE) to +0.8 V (SCE) were recorded in As2O3 aqueous phase, including and without the accumulation step at 0.8 V (SCE) as shown in Fig. S5. When the accumulation phase was disregarded, the first signal at about 0.2 V (SCE) was not recognized, but the second signal at around +0.4 V (SCE) was. Furthermore, when dilutions of the As2O3 solution were introduced and ASV scans were taken, only the oxidation peak at +0.4 V (SCE) was seen, which increased as anticipated. According to these findings, the first peak was caused by the oxidation of As(0) to As(III), while the second signal was caused by the oxidation of As(III) to As(V). In addition, As(III) was not reduced if the aggregation phase was not conducted at the commencement of the analysis, and its oxidization peak was not identified in the subsequent anodic scan. Fig. S5a shows that the PCA-rGO/AuNPs electrodes had the same intensity of anodic peaks as the AuNPs electrodes at 0.2 V. Because only Au NPs catalyzes the oxidation of As(0), as shown in Fig. S5a, and PCA-rGO is not electroactive, the fact that PCA-rGO/Au NPs electrodes produced the same results as AuNPs with a lower concentration of NPs highlights the beneficial effects of PCA-induced NPs-rGO electron coupling, which contributed to increasing faradaic current. The LODs in the concentration range of 10−7 M to 10−4 M were determined after the sensitivity of the modified electrodes against As(III) detection was examined. For the C-SPEs modified with PCA-rGO/Au NPs, AuNPs, and PCA-rGO@AuNPs, calibration curves were acquired. The ASV scans of the PCA-rGO/AuNPs electrodes after additional administrations of As2O3 in solution have been shown in Fig. S5b. The calibration curve derived from the findings has been shown in the inset.

Sapner and Sathe (2021) employed a metal-free tyramine-functionalized graphene oxide (T-GO) electrocatalyst to measure ascorbic acid (AA) and an organic pollutant specifically substituted by a phenolic molecule, p-nitrophenol, in a 1 M phosphate buffer solution at pH 7 as a model species. In biosensing and industrial waste management, the CV investigation of AA and p-nitrophenol proved crucial. Thus, deploying the T-GO-modified GC electrode for AA oxidation at an initial potential of −0.03 V vs. SCE, electrochemical measurement of AA and p-nitrophenol was successful. A redox process for the detection of p-nitrophenol began at an initial potential oxidation peak (O1) of 0.047 V vs. SCE and reduction peaks (R1 and R2) of −0.62 V vs. SCE and 0.147 V vs. SCE, respectively. For the sensing of both AA and p-nitrophenol, the T-GO-based electrocatalyst had outstanding durability and repeatability, which could be attributed to its enhanced active surface area after GO was functionalized with tyramine.

In another experiment, Ercarikci and Alanyalioglu (2021) used consecutive vacuum-filtration and hydrothermal reduction methods to develop a dual-functional and flexible rGO based paper-like material including ZnO-NPs and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA). After coating GO films with ZnO-NPs, EDTA was immobilized via ultrasonic treatment. To manufacture GO/ZnO-NPs-EDTA paper, a generated GO/ZnO-NPs-EDTA suspension was vacuum-filtered on a membrane and peeled. Hydrothermal reduction produced rGO/ZnO-NPs-EDTA paper, which was used for the first time in two applications: Simultaneous removal of Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ heavy metal ions and subsequent electrochemical detection. The rGO/ZnO-NPs-EDTA paper's maximum adsorption efficiency was ascertained to be 2963, 8056, 600, and 1753 mg/g, respectively, whereas electrochemical simultaneous estimation demonstrated linear ranges of 18.5–500, 22.4–700, 8.3–200, 3.3–300 nM and LODs values of 5.6, 6.8, 2.5, and 1.0 nM for Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, and Hg2+.

Based on a π-π stacking interaction among the graphene and indole, Lin et al. (2021) used a graphene field-effect transistor (G-FET) to identify the volatile molecule-indole. When indole was applied to G-FET devices, the electrical signal shifted due to the indole molecule adsorbing onto the graphene surface via π-π stacking. Fig. S6 depicts the concept of a G-FET-based metabolic volatile indole detector (Lin et al., 2021).

In the above experiment, the indole's adsorption produced a charge rearrangement in the graphene-indole compound, resulting in alterations in the electrical signal of G-FET biosensors varying indole concentrations. The indole biosensor detected indole at concentrations of 10 ppb to 250 ppb, with a LOD of 10 ppb for indole solution detection. Fig. 4a shows the analytical setup for detecting indole in the gas phase. The back gate had been used, and the gate voltage was transmitted directly to the backside of the silicon. Two tubules were attached to the indole solid in a plastic test tube. A syringe was linked to the short tubule, which was actuated by a syringe pump. The G-FET biosensor was covered with a long tubule. The syringe pump was set to 200 L/min throughout the detection operation in order for the volatile indole to approach the G-FET biosensor surface. The studies were carried out at a temperature of 20 °C. Because E. coli generated a variety of metabolites that interacted with indole detection, like several forms of alcohols and alcohol esters, they investigated into alcohol and ketones interference tests to see how the carbonyl and hydroxy groups affected the results. Both ketones and alcohol caused minor variations in the electrical signal, but once the sensor was no longer exposed to alcohols and ketones, the signal change dissipated (Fig. 4b, c). The signal change caused by indole remained steady even after the pump was turned off.

Following into the experiment, as the indole gas detection condition was underneath an atmosphere, a reference experiment utilizing air gas was carried out, as illustrated in Fig. 4e. The real-time finding showed that there was no discernible difference in the electrical characteristics of graphene before and after air interacts with it. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 4d, the transfer curve was characterized. The black line scarcely altered as compared to the bare graphene transfer curve (red line), showing that the electrical signal was untouched by airflow. Real-time detection of indole gas was carried out at a gate voltage of 25 V. The source-drain current (△Ids) increased dramatically when the syringe pump was turned on, as seen in Fig. 4f. However, when the pump was switched off, the △Ids remained consistent. The real-time response for gas-phase indole detection was flat about 81.5 A and resulted in a signal shift of 13.4 A, suggesting a reaction of 19.7%. Because of the π-π interactions linked to its molecular structure, it was accumulated on a graphene surface, causing a signal shift in G-FETs. This research demonstrated that the G-FET sensor can detect indole gas and several other toxic gases. Table S2 includes the several graphene-based NMs biosensors encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — High specific surface area, extraordinary electronic properties, electron transport capabilities and ultrahigh flexibility.
Disadvantages — No standardization in production and difficulties due to the high agglomeration tendency during production

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have attracted people's interest since Lijima and his colleagues first revealed their amazing physiochemical capabilities in 1992 (Xie et al., 2019). After that, there was a lot of research on CNTs manufacturing, structure, and characteristics, which contributed to their evolution. Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNs) are the two forms of carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). SWCNTs are a single graphite sheet wrapped with a tube diameter of 1–2 nm, whereas MWCNTs are circumferential, enclosed graphite tubules with widths of 2–50 nm and an interlayer distance of roughly 0.34 nm. SWCNTs often have an open-ended nanostraw or a capped nanohorn tubular form. Because of their highly oriented geometries, these novel nanostructures exhibit electrical, thermal, chemical and mechanical characteristics which are unique from bulk diamond and graphite. SWCNTs and MWCNTs are made using a variety of methods (for example, chemical vapor deposition (CVD)). Carbon atoms in CNTs with sp2 hybridization have a higher elastic modulus and tensile strength than those with sp3 hybridization (Hernandez-Vargas et al., 2018; Sha et al., 2018). Furthermore, CNTs have unique electrical properties due to π bonds created by π electron orbital overlap. CNTs have a huge surface area, which means they have a lot of catalytic activity. Because of these advantages, CNTs can be widely used in the field of electrochemical biosensors to detect a variety of EPs. However, in hydrophilic solvents, CNTs are insoluble and prefer to clump together. To overcome these difficulties, nanocomposites including polymers, ionic liquids, or metal NPs have been designed to improve electrocatalytic properties and/or ease bioreceptor immobilization. Carbon nanofibers (CNFs), a type of CNT, are most often used in the construction of biosensors due to their unique features. CNTs could enhance the electroactivity of biomolecules and improve the electron-transfer process of proteins due to their electrocatalytic capabilities (Bhanjana et al., 2018; Hui et al., 2018; Power et al., 2018). Because of these properties, CNTs are important in a wide variety of electrochemical biosensors, from amperometric enzyme electrodes to DNA hybridization biosensors and immunosensors.

Taking forward the study of CNT-based biosensors, Chen et al. (2021) created an Au@MWCNTs nanocomposite with a modified GCE (Au@MWCNTs/GCE) used as a dichlorvos pesticides sensor in agriculture. The face-centered cubic configuration of AuNPs was generated and uniformly dispersed over the network-like frame of MWCNTs, displaying remarkable permeability, wide effective surface region, and rapid electron transport efficiency of Au@MWCNTs/GCE, as revealed by SEM and XRD investigations. The electrochemical characterization of Au@MWCNTs/GCE was examined using DPV and CV, revealing that the dichlorvos sensor's LODs and linear range was 5 nM and 1–120 M, respectively. Due to the synergistic effects of Au nanoparticles and MWCNTs, comparison of the obtained electrode using various comparable stated sensors indicated a lowered LODs and acceptable linear range for dichlorvos, which is equivalent to other gold and carbon nanostructured based sensors. In the context of several species, dichlorvos sensors were also studied. The analytical applicability of the dichlorvos sensor was investigated using stimulated leaf lettuce samples, which revealed appropriate retrieval values ranging from 90.75% to 97.50% and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.18% to 4.85%, indicating that Au@MWCNTs/GCE was relevant for detecting dichlorvos in real samples.

Rabai et al. (2022) published a study on a label-free electrochemical aptasensor for water cadmium detection. A nanocomposite using AuNPs, CNTs, and chitosan (Cs) as immobilization support for the Cd aptamer was produced for this purpose. CNTs-Cs were first utilized to optimize the GCE surface. Then, employing chrono-amperometry, AuNPs were coated on CNTs-Cs/GCE. Finally, glutaraldehyde cross-linking was used to immobilize the amino-modified Cd-aptamer. For the manufacturing of the aptamer/CNTs/AuNPs modified GCE surface, a droplet of 0.5 mg/mL CNTs– Cs was introduced to GCE and dried in the air (1 mg of CNTs diffused in 2.0 mL of 0.2% Cs solution). After that, AuNPs were electrodeposited on a CNTs–Cs modified electrode using potentiostatic electrodeposition of 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution containing 1 mM of HAuCl4 for 400 s at 0.2 V. After the NPs were electrodeposited, the modified electrode was thoroughly cleaned in water and air-dried at ambient temperature. For aptamer immobilization, 6 L of Cs solution (0.2%) was applied to the AuNPs/CNTs/CS film and air-dried at room temperature. After 2 h in a 0.25% glutaraldehyde solution, the modified electrode was incubated on the surface with 5 l of aptamer solution overnight at 4 °C.

Following the experiment, EIS was used to assess the proposed biosensor's capability for cadmium measurement. Under ideal conditions, the aptamer-modified surface was subjected to increased quantities of Cd. The introduction of Cd to the modified electrode surface resulted in a considerable improvement in the diameter of the Nyquist circle. As shown in Fig. S7a, increasing Cd2+ concentration in a logarithmic manner increased charge transfer resistance (Rct) values (Rabai et al., 2022). The first semi-circle of the Nyquist plot represents the aptamer-modified surface before the addition of cadmium. Following processing with rising concentrations of the target, the Nyquist plot semi-circles continued to increase, indicating that cadmium and its specific aptamer interact.

Randles equivalent circuit model (Fig. S7b) was used to fit Nyquist plot semi-circles, wherein Rs denotes the solution resistance, Q2 denotes the constant phase element (an equivalent model of double-layer capacitance). As Cd concentration expanded, the real portion of impedance (Re(Z)) increased. The results showed that the higher the Rct, the more Cd2+ was trapped on the aptasensing platform. As a function logarithm of Cd concentration, the linear regression equation was standardized to Rct = Rct (aptamer‑cadmium)–Rct (aptamer). Rct (aptamer‑cadmium) was the electron transfer resistance after Cd2+ attaches to the immobilized aptamer on the modified electrode surface. Rct denotes the value of the electron transfer resistance after aptamer immobilization on the modified electrode surface (aptamer). A continuous linear association between Rct and Cd concentrations was established in the concentration range of 10−13–10−4 M, with an excellent correlation coefficient of (R2 = 0.985). The LOD of the aptasensor was 2.0–10 nM, which was calculated as three times the “s/m” ratio, where “s” is the standard deviation of the blank impedance signal (three replicates) and “m” is the slope of the appropriate calibration curve. The suggested biosensor demonstrated decent Cd detection capability at a low applied voltage (0.5 V), having a LOD of 0.02 nM, and a large linear range (10−13–10−4 nM. Furthermore, the biosensor demonstrated high specificity against interfering ions such as Pb2+, Hg2+, and Zn2+.

Jia et al. (2022) created a dual-channel fluorescence probe (SiCDs@mMIPs-cit-Eu) by combining two independent reaction sites in a nanomaterial, one with a SiCDs-doped mesoporous silica molecular imprinting group and the other with a europium complex group. The synergism of the inner filter effect and the “antenna effect” ensured that the difference between Tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) was resolved. Furthermore, with great specificity and sensitivity, this innovative method identified TC and OTC in buffer solution and real samples consecutively. This approach indicated better reaction to TC and OTC with LODs of 5 and 16 nM, respectively, for concentrations ranging from 0 to 5.5 M. The compact and inexpensive paper-based sensor was developed to provide multi-color visual on-site detection of TC and OTC when used in conjunction with a smartphone color-scanning tool. To investigate the outstanding sensitivity capabilities of SiCDs@mMIP-cit-Eu for the detection of TC and OTC, fluorescence titration and visual fluorescence imaging studies were carried out (Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5a demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of SiCDs@mMIP-cit-Eu at 616 nm grew considerably after varying amounts of TC were introduced, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm steadily decreased. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I616/I450) was shown to be proportional to TC concentrations in a significant way. Fig. 5b shows that the fluorescence ratio I616/I450 displayed two linear relationships with TC concentrations in the ranges of 10 nM to 1.5 M and 1.5 to 5.5 M, with LODs of about 5 nM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The chromaticity diagram of the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) depicted the change in fluorescence emission of SiCDs@mMIP-cit-Eu at various TC concentrations (Fig. 5c). It was clearly visible under the UV lamp that when the TC concentration increased, the fluorescence color of the detecting device changed from blue to red (Fig. 5d).

Naik et al. (2021) used a pulsed laser-assisted approach accompanied by a wet chemical process to create unique ZnS/Au/f-MWCNT nanostructures. Pulsed laser ablation of a Zn target in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), which was utilized as a solvent and sulfur source, was utilized to make ZnS nanospheres. There were no additional sulfur sources, surfactants, or reducing agents used during the synthesis. The devised electrochemical sensor based on ZnS/Au/f-MWCNT nano-composites rapidly and selectively detected a dangerous contaminant, 4-nitrophenol. According to LSV investigation, the enhanced ZnS/Au10/f-MWCNT3 nanocomposite displayed a wide linear dynamic response (10–150 M), high sensitivity (0.8084 A/M.cm−2), and low LOD (30 nM). The presence of many active sites (electrochemical surface area = 0.00369 F cm−2) and a quicker electron transfer rate were attributed to the enhanced electrode's superior 4-nitrophenol sensing capabilities. The selective detection of 4-nitrophenol was not hampered by a 100-fold excess of competing ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cl, NO3, 4-nitrophenol, AA, and 2-NP). The newly developed ZnS/Au10/f-MWCNT3 nanocomposite proved to be an efficient sensor for the detection of harmful organic nitro compounds in a selective and sensitive manner. Table S3 includes the several CNT-based NMs-biosensors encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — High electrical conductivity, high flexibility and strength.
Disadvantages — Lower lifetime

Enzymes are macromolecules that operate as biological catalysts and have a sophisticated 3D configuration made up of proteins. A biological sensing element in an enzyme-based biosensor is a specific enzyme, which is paired with a transducer that translates the signal produced by the enzymatic reaction into a measured response proportional to the analyte concentration. Thermal release, oxygen release or absorption, proton concentration changes, light emission or absorption, and other types of enzyme reaction signals could all be generated. This signal is transformed by the transducer (optical, thermal, electrochemical, piezoelectric) into current, potential, light absorption, temperature exchange and so on all of which are detectable in different ways. Enzymatic biosensors centered on NMs are now commonly used in food safety, environmental monitoring and medical services. Because it influences the durability and enzymatic response duration of enzyme-based biosensors, immobilization of enzymes within transducers is critical (Gai et al., 2019a; Kuswandi, 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019). As enzymes include numerous functional groups like carboxylic (–COOH), thiol (–SH), amino (−NH2), and others, they may readily be attached simply onto nanoparticles. Enzyme adsorbents could be NMs having hydrophobic or charged regions on their surfaces that can interface with enzymes or NMs with chemical groups that could adhere to the appropriate enzyme groups. Inorganic mesoporous materials, such as zeolites and mesoporous silicon spheres, are interesting adsorbents because they have a vast surface area of the crystal and could absorb diverse chemical groups. NMs can also be manufactured individually and then combined with the enzyme solution before being immobilized. This method could be utilized to incorporate NMs that do not bind to the enzymes. Several NMs (particularly organic ones) do not disintegrate or readily agglomerate in aqueous solutions, necessitating the inclusion of surfactants or other stabilizers to the resultant NM/enzyme mixture. Auxiliary chemicals must be chosen with caution, as they have the potential to reduce enzyme activity. It is feasible to integrate both NM incorporation methods by first immobilizing the enzyme on NM and then connecting the enzyme/NM composite to the electrode. Pesticides (e.g., phenolic compounds) and NOx chemicals have been detected using enzyme- or protein-based NPs sensors in pollution monitoring. The efficient immobilization and “electrical attachment” of enzymes to solid electrode surfaces is a fundamental problem in developing such sensitive and stable sensors. Fig. S8 shows the working principle of an NMs based enzymatic biosensor. Here, electrodes are connected to biological sensing elements (Hernandez-Vargas et al., 2018). Enzyme-nanomaterial electrochemical sensors integrate the identification and catalytic characteristics of enzymes with the electronic properties of NMs. NMs can also improve enzyme immobilization procedures, promote electron transfers, and improve the selectivity of bio catalyzed reactions by incorporating them into the design of biosensors (Coroş et al., 2018; Jin and Maduraiveeran, 2018; Samsidar et al., 2018). In the availability of the tested pesticide, the pesticide analyte inhibits enzymatic components and reduces electrochemical reactions, that is how they work. As a result, in indirect detection, the reaction in the addition of an analyte is evaluated to the reaction in the exclusion of an analyte. Additionally, because most NMs contain charges, they may electrostatically adsorb biomolecules having opposing charges or adhere to active regions on biomolecules' interfaces. Because enzyme active sites are surrounded by a protein shell, effective electrical contact with the electrode substrates is infrequent. The electron exchange between the active centers and electrodes is impeded. The conductivity of NMs improves the frequency of simultaneous electron transmission between the enzyme's active site and electrodes that act as electrical wires. These convert the signal into a readable format for better analysis.

Various forms of NPs have shown biocompatibility with enzymes, including Pt-NPs, Au-NPs and Ag-NPs. They also have a number of advantages over optical sensors, including a minimal price, small structure, biodegradable origin, and the possibility to combine several sensing elements into a single chip-like sensor. In a research, Chauhan and Pundir (2012) described a technique for developing an extremely sensitive electrochemical biosensor for the detection of malathion, monocrotophos, chlorpyrifos and endosulfan based on covalent immobilization of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) on Fe3O4NPs-decorated carboxylated-MWCNTs) electrodeposited onto indium. The biosensor displayed a linear response for acetylthiocholine in the concentration range of 0.1–700 mol L−1 and a substantial sensitivity for acetylthiocholine of 0.402 mA/mol L−1. For malathion, chlorpyrifos, monocrotophos, and endosulfan, the inhibition rates were proportionate to their concentrations ranging of 0.1–70 nmol L−1, 0.1–50 nmol L−1, 0.1–70 nmol L−1, and 0.1–100 nmol L−1, correspondingly, under optimal circumstances. The biosensor had a LOD of 0.1 nmol L−1 for all pesticides with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The biosensor had excellent repeatability, no metal ion interference, and long-term durability. The current biosensor's measurement results were quite similar to those acquired using the traditional gas chromatography–mass spectrometry approach. Pesticides were detected using the biosensor in both ambient and dietary samples.

Similarly, Han et al. (2007)) created a glucose biosensor that was very specific and sensitive by immobilizing glucose oxidase within a mesoporous CNT–titania–Nafion composite film deposited on a platinized GCE. The accurate and consistent quantification of glucose was enabled by the direct reduction of enzymatically-liberated H2O2 at −0.1 V versus Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl) without the need of a mediator (3 M NaCl). The described biosensor performed linearly to glucose throughout a vast concentration range of 5.0 × 10−5 to 5.0 × 10−3 M, with a sensibility of 154 mA M−1 cm−2. Because of the mesoporous nature of the CNT– titania–Nafion composite film, the current biosensor had a reaction time of fewer than 2 s. Furthermore, enormous uric acid and ascorbic acid excesses did not affect the investigated biosensor.

Nogueira Pedroza Dias Mello and Bueno (2020) compared enzymatic biosensors' label-free electrochemical response to that of their visual counterpart. They correlated the electrochemical impedimetric and first-ever documented capacitive responses of enzyme biosensors to their optical reflectance responses as a proof-of-concept. In this research, detection of glucose and urea was the goal. The sensors' chemical platform was made up of enzymatically functionalized polyaniline thin sheets. For both electrochemical and optical experimental conditions, linearity, sensitivity, and the LOD were investigated. They discovered that the impedimetric/capacitive electrochemical setup produced a response of similar reliability to the optical response (sensitivities of 10.7, 7.4, and 4.3% per decade for impedimetric, capacitors, and optical glucose biosensors), but including a wider linear range (10−4 to 10−1 mol L−1 for both glucose and urea biosensors) and a similar LOD value.

Similarly, Li et al. (2020) effectively enclosed laccase (LAC) in ZIF-8 and connected it with the skeleton of bacterial cellulose (BC)/carboxylated(c)-MWCNTs to generate an extremely flexible BC/c-MWCNTs/ZIF-8@LAC electrode. They then devised a highly adaptable self-powered sensing platform centered on a biofuel-cell operated by a single enzyme, with BPA as a model analyte. For BPA concentrations, the maximum power density (Pmax) of a single-enzymatic biofuel cell (EBFC) showed an outstanding linear dynamic range of 0.01 to 0.4 mM, exhibiting a minimal LODs of 1.95 × 10−3 mM. As a result, the developed single-EBFC-based self-powered sensor proved successful in detecting BPA and provides a wide spectrum of potential applications in the domain of water pollutants detection along with several other EPs. The Table S4 includes the several NMs based enzymatic biosensors encompassing the pollutant, target, LOD, LOQ/sensitivity, various evaluation techniques used for the determination of the biosensor efficiency towards EPs detection and remediation.
Advantages — High sensitivity and specificity, portability, cost-effectiveness.
Disadvantages — Signal diminution from fouling compounds and interruption from chemicals existing in the sample matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other crucial types of Nanomaterials based biosensors for the detection of EPs
Layered double hydroxides (LDH) based electrochemical sensors
ce:section.ce:para: LDHs are 2D nanostructured compounds having a variety of physicochemical features. They are a fascinating class of inorganic substances exhibiting a broad range of chemical compositions and morphologies. They are also known as anionic clays or hydrotalcite. They are made up of layers of negatively charged metal hydroxide trapped between charge-balancing anions and water molecules. LDHs possess a similar structure as brucite, Mg(OH)2, where Mg2+ is surrounded by six OH ions and the resulting octahedral frameworks are linked to form an infinite two-dimensional layer (Farzin et al., 2018; Goel and Arora, 2018; Manikandan et al., 2018). By substituting some divalent ions with trivalent ions, brucite layers became positively charged. By locating anions in the interlayer voids, the positive charge is neutralized or balanced. The complexation of water molecules in the interlayer gaps stabilizes the architecture of the resultant LDHs. Because of electrostatic contact and hydrogen bonding between the layer and interlayer components, the LDH architecture is stable. LDH structures can be fine-tuned by modifying divalent and trivalent ions, as well as intercalated anions. The radii of the indicated divalent and trivalent ions must be comparable to Mg2+ and Al3+. LDHs are moderate, non-toxic, and have a huge surface coverage. They also have a two-dimensional architecture, a positively charged surface, recyclable intercalated anions and a programmable internally and externally design. Because of their porous architecture, substantial anion exchange capabilities, and water-resistant architectures, they offer special applications. Catalysis, flame retardants, fuel cells, drug administration, adsorbent, and analytical extraction methods are just a few of the applications for LDHs.

LDHs are utilized in the production of enzyme biosensors for environmental pollutants due to
•
Sintering resistance
•
Enhanced metal distribution
•
Small particle diameter
•
Relatively large regions
•
Thermal stability is outstanding
•
Diffusion resistance is decreased due to a more uniform dispersion of active species
•
LDHs/metal or metal oxide composites

On LDH-modified electrodes, metals could be electrodeposited. LDH serves as a relatively stable matrix for metal electrodeposition. On LDH-modified GCE, electrodeposition could be used to construct Ag dendritic nanostructures without the use of a template or surfactant. These depositions provide users with more control over the volume and shape of the emerging metallic frameworks. Changes in the input voltage, electrolysis time and precursor concentration all influence it. As a result, metallic structures that are perceptive, robust, and uniformly dispersed are produced. Electrocatalytic activity for detecting certain analytes is more influenced by the geometry of metallic surfaces. For electrochemical sensing, LDHs are combined alongside metal oxides and nanostructures. MnO intercalated LDHs, LDHs encased around CuO nanoparticles, and LDHs comprising a Fe3O4 nanoparticle core are all common. These pairings resulted in an architecturally interconnected hybrid with p-type semi-conductive LDH channels and narrow bandgap metal oxide NPs. CuO@MnAl NSs, for instance, have an intrinsic p-n junction, which could improve electron kinetics at a p-n junction diode's breakdown voltage and hence increase electrocatalytic activity. In a research, Baig and Sajid (2017) explored the use of enzymatic biosensors based on LDH for detecting contaminants in the environment. They revealed that LDHs are enticing elements for electrode remodeling because of their minimal price, convenience of production, biocompatibility, and ease of catalyst loading. Furthermore, the LDHs have tuneable properties due to their composition flexibility. The immobilization of the electrocatalyst was an intriguing characteristic that improved the LDH's selectivity and sensitivity to specific environmental pollutants. In addition, the loaded electrocatalyst in LDH aided the created sensor in dealing with potential interferences. Heavy metals, pesticides, polyphenols, and other harmful inorganic chemicals were detected using LDH-modified electrodes. Among the several innovative modifying materials for sensing environmental contaminants, LDH modified sensors have a lot of promise.

Prof. Gogotsi and colleagues introduced MXenes, a distinct class of 2D transition metal nitrides, carbides, or carbonitrides, to the 2D NMs group in 2011 (Kalambate et al., 2019). MXenes are produced by removing substrate A from initial MAX phases selectively, where M stands for an intermediate transition metal, A for a group A element, and X for C or N. The MAX aspect is a thick multilayer composition composed of consecutive M and A films with a strong metallic covalent MeX link and a weak MeA bond. MAX components have the capacity to conduct electricity and heat, as well as have chemical resistance and a low thermal expansion coefficient. MXenes have attracted considerable attention in the broader scientific world and enterprises since their discovery, owing to their distinctive layered morphological features, high conductivity, exemplary hydrophilicity, high thermal stability, high surface area and eco-friendly attributes (Hondred et al., 2018; Parnianchi et al., 2018). MXene nanostructures are used to manufacture a wide range of sensing equipment in recent years, including electrochemical (bio) sensors, gas adsorptive sensors, optical sensors and piezoresistive wearable sensors. MXenes, like graphene and other 2D materials, may incorporate additional elements that increase structural and electrical properties, such as metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, enzymes and conducting polymers. In a research, excluding the external reducing agents, one-step hybridization of Ag, Au and PdNPs from their aqueous formulations onto the titanium carbode (Ti3C2Tx) MXene interface was achieved and employed as a substrate for surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). Furthermore, by reducing the Pt precursor in situ on the MXene surface, a Ti3C2TxePtNP composite was generated and employed for electrochemical sensing. The MXene PtNP nanocomposite outperforms MXene alone in terms of electrochemical responsiveness due to its improved catalytic activity, high conductivity and wide surface area (Ba Hashwan et al., 2020). Furthermore, Kalambate et al. (2019) offered a summary of existing state-of-the-art advances in the domain of electrochemical (bio) sensors for biomarker, pharmaceutical medication, and EPs detection. In the beginning, they looked at how different synthetic techniques and surface functionalization alter MXene characteristics and sensing efficiency. MXenes were extensively studied as an electrode modification for detecting pharmacological compounds, ecological pollutants, vital illness biomarkers, and other important disease biomarkers. They revealed that electrochemical (bio) sensors based on MXenes are capable of detecting a wide spectrum of analytes. However, MXene sensors have LODs in the picomolar to fempto molar range for ecological pollutants, which is much better than typical detection techniques like chromatography and spectroscopy.

For the detection and monitoring of bromate (BrO3) in water, Rasheed et al. (2018) created a modular and ultra-sensitive sensor technology premised on a lamellar Ti3C2Tx (MXene)-modified GCE. MXene was shown to possess distinctive electrocatalytic capabilities for effective BrO3 reduction in this investigation, and the produced sensor had a linear response for BrO3 concentrations ranging from 50 nM to 5 mM, exhibiting a LOD of 41 nM. A rise in peak current and a shift to lower potential were considered to indicate the catalytic reduction of BrO3 by MXene. SEM, TEM, XRD were used to evaluate surface alterations to MXene after electrocatalytic reduction of BrO3. Following the electrochemical reduction of BrO3, the production of trace TiO2 crystals on the surface of Ti3C2Tx verified the redox reaction among BrO3 and MXene. Among other interfering ions, the devised sensor showed remarkable selectivity for BrO3. This study established MXene as a viable platform for detecting water pollutants, particularly when its redox activity was used.

The hydrolysis of ACh, a neurotransmitter ester, is catalyzed by the AChE enzyme family. The reaction ACh + H2O + choline + acetate is catalyzed by AChE. Organophosphates block AChE by phosphorylating a serine residue in the enzyme's active region, causing it to become inactive. The phosphorylated enzyme, which is extremely persistent, prevents ACh hydrolysis. OP insecticides and neurological toxins have been detected using AChE biosensors. In majority AChE-based biosensors, OP-pesticide detection relied on the suppression of AChE function. The enzymatic products oxidation or reduction currents are employed to monitor AChE activity in this system. The degree of chemical inhibitor existing in the system has an inverse relationship with AChE activity. The sensor could be used to monitor chemicals that are known to impede AChE activity in blood, saliva, or tissue because more inhibitors equal less enzyme activity. To enhance the stability and response in trace-pesticide detection, AChE has been immobilized onto a range of NMs-modified surfaces in recent decades, including CNTs, graphene, AuNPs, and QDs. For instance, Wang et al. (2011) created a nanohybrid comprising GO nanosheets and AuNPs were chemically reduced. Then, for ultrasensitive detection of paraoxon, an electrochemical sensor based on AChE/AuNPs/GO was designed. Paraoxon was synthesized by self-assembling AChE upon an AuNPs/GO nanohybrid. The nanosize impact of AuNPs dispersed over GO sheets enhanced electrochemical detection of enzymatically produced thiocholine, a putative negative transition in operation (−380 mV), considerable electrochemical signal attenuation, and improved stability and sensitivity. Paraoxon levels as low as μM were detected by the biosensor. Furthermore, Liu and Lin (2006) detected paraoxon on a CNT-modified electrode using self-assembled AChE. The linearity was observed up to 1.6 mM, and the LOD was 0.4 μM. QDs and AuNPs, in addition to carbon materials, are widely utilized in the construction of biosensors due to their distinctive attributes, which create a favorable environment for biomolecule immobilization while maintaining bioactivity. Additionally, using an AChE biosensor, Du et al. (2008) immobilized AChE onto a GCE surface-enhanced with cadmium telluride (CdTe) QDs/AuNPs. With a LOD of 1.34 nM, monocrotophos inhibition was proportionate to its concentration in two ranges: 0.005–4.48 M and 0.009–0.067 M. These findings showed that combining AuNPs and CdTe QDs increased the sensitivity of detection by promoting AChE electron transfer and catalyzing the electron-oxidation of thiocholine.

Sahub et al. (2018) created a pesticide sensor using GQDs and an active enzyme, dubbed the GQDs/enzyme platform, to detect organophosphate pesticides. In this notion, H2O2 produced by the active enzymatic activity of choline oxidase (CHOx) and AChE reacts with GQDs, causing the photoluminescence of GQDs to be “turned off.” In the presence of organophosphate, GQDs showed “turn-on” photo-luminescence at 467 nm. Clearly, the photoluminescence variations of the GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor were proportional to the pesticide concentration. The LOD of GQDs/AChE/CHOx biosensor for dichlorvos was 0.172 ppm (0.778 μM). This biosensor provided a potential assessment of organophosphate pesticides as well as an advantage for quick testing of organophosphate pesticides in meals, groundwater, and the environment with cheap cost, ease of preparation, and low environmental toxicity.
OPH is an enzyme that hydrolyzes parathion, paraoxon, coumaphos, and methyl parathion, among other OP pesticides. The following is the basic reaction that OPH catalyzes:

The enzyme may hydrolyze PO, PS and P-CN bonds, causing a pH shift that could be monitored. The hydrolysis intermediates might be monitored electrochemically or spectrophotometrically, allowing the analyte to be detected quickly because the volume of product production is directly proportional to the amount of analyte. This technique's precise counterpart is the inhibition-based enzymatic detection technique employed with AChE. The aforementioned OPH enzyme reaction has been aggregated with a wide range of transduction strategies to generate OPH biosensors for specific quantitative measurement of OP contaminants in the surroundings, despite the fact that AChE inhibition biosensors are not specific for OP pesticides, despite the fact that AChE is a benchmark for carbamate pesticides. In a research, Zhao et al. (2013) focused on the self of methyl parathion hydrolase (MPH) on the Fe3O4@Au nanocomposite created an OPH biosensor for detecting methyl parathion. The enzyme biosensor was readily constructed by applying an external magnetic field to the magnetic nanocomposite, and the electrode surface could be replenished by removing the magnet. The biosensor showed a quick response and excellent selectivity for detecting methyl parathion under ideal conditions, exhibiting a linear range of 0.5–1000 ng/mL and a LOD of 0.1 ng/mL. As a transduction method, fluorescence detection provided great sensitivity, minimal LODs, and a wide detection spectrum. Leblanc's lab also presented a novel biosensor for the detection of paraoxon based on (CdSe)ZnS core-shell QDs and an OPH bioconjugate. Electrostatic interactions between the positively charged side-chain and negatively charged QD surfaces, as well as end groups (NH2) of protein, were used to attach the OPH to (CdSe)ZnS core-shell QDs. Under the influence of paraoxon, the fluorescence intensity of the OPH-QD bioconjugates was attenuated. The conformational alteration of the enzyme influenced the quenching process, changing the level of surface passivation of the QDs. This biosensor exhibited a LOD of roughly 10 nM for paraoxon. To create a polyelectrolyte architecture, they employed OPH polycations and chitosan, as well as thioglycolic acid-capped CdSe QDs.

Although immune-based pesticide detection is not as accurate as enzymatic biosensors, the first findings stretch back over 25 years. The construction of a multichannel sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid having a LOD of 0.0018 nM was described in one of the early studies. The flow injection approach provided the same LOD for 2,4,5-trichlorophenoxyacetic acid and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, but the potentiometric enzymatic immunoassay yielded a LOD of 0.089 nM. To detect atrazine, researchers employed glycine-doped polyaniline nanofilms and biotinylated-Fab fragment K47 antibody on Si alongside anti-atrazine antibodies. The LOD was reported in g per mL. One LOD of 9.31 × 10−8 M was obtained using impedimetric scans using a graphene-modified screen-printed immunosensor. The usage of GQDs resulted in additional improvements in LOD and a reduction of 8.23 × 10−8 M. The LOD of atrazine utilizing electrospun Mn2O3 nanofibers impedimetric detection was, nevertheless, the most amazing. Later on, antibodies to atrazine have been added to the mix. The LOD was only 3.93 × 10−15 nM utilizing this method. Microorganisms were also utilized as platforms for detecting pesticides electrochemically. Fig. S9 represents a schematic representation of electrochemical immunosensor (Zhang et al., 2019b).

This is also a novel strategy. The primary concept was to evaluate the metabolites of pesticide metabolism as well as pesticides' detrimental impacts on living cells. Given the complexity of live cells and their sensitivities to individual compounds, these systems appeared to be particularly well suited to monitoring aggregate contamination. Amperometric detection was linked to a framework formed of Saccharomyces cerevisiae cells upon a composite of sodium alginate and polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel as a substrate at the electrode surface to investigate the toxicity of several EPs such as ametryn, acephate, and thiram. To monitor water contamination, Shewanella oneidensis, Escherichia coli, and Methylosinus trichosporium, OB3b were immobilized on a screen-printed Au electrode surface. At the same period, Abu-Ali et al. (2019) emphasized the concept of sensor arrays incorporating sensors. Pesticides (atrazine), petrochemicals, and heavy metal ions were all detected utilizing a recently developed artificial neural network technology that uses bacteria immobilized on Au screen-printed electrodes to detect contaminants.

Through coordination interactions among metal ions/clusters and carbon-containing organic ligands, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with intramolecular pores self-assemble as an organic-inorganic hybrid material. MOFs can be synthesized using hydrothermal methods, sonochemical methods, microwave techniques, mechanochemical synthesis methods and electrochemical methods. The diversity of ligands, metal ions and structures, and also the capacity to post-synthetically modify them, expand the scope of functional MOF materials synthesis. MOFs have aroused a lot of interest in sensing of various EPs, drug delivery, proton conduction, supercapacitors and transformation because of their huge surface area, customizable chemical architectures and ultrahigh porosity (up to 90% free volume). As a result, most MOFs exhibit fluorescence properties when exposed to UV or visible light, which could be attributed to the charge transfer between metal ions and organic ligands, as well as organic ligands in aromatic subunits and lanthanide (Ln) metal cations in Ln-MOFs. MOFs have gained popularity as optical sensing materials due to their photoluminescence. The majority of the research has gone into developing sensors for detecting several pesticides and pollutants. The practical usefulness of MOF photo-luminescence materials in EPs analysis has been discussed in this work, with a focus on MOF materials exhibiting beneficial properties and MOF-based sensor systems. In a research, Tajik et al. (2021a) reviewed the most recent electrochemical detection techniques for environmental toxins employing MOFs for a variety of environmental specimens. They claimed that combining MOFs comprising of several conductive materials, including metal NPs, polymers, carbon nanostructures and other materials, can increase conductance. Furthermore, 2D MOF nanosheets have attracted a lot of survey attention as a new class of porous materials owing to their intriguing chemistry and impressive attributes such as ultrathin thickness, large specific surface area, configurable framework, high aspect ratio, high porosity and available active regions. Their unique features arising from the coupling of 2D structure and NMs with MOFs offered promise for boosting electrocatalytic behavior because of the rapid charge transfer and enhanced quantity of surface-active sites. Additionally, 2D MOF nanosheets conduct electricity better than bulk 3D MOFs, making them more useful in electrochemistry. As a result, more investigation on the unique 2D conductive MOFs was anticipated, with a calculated value of 3.05 × 10−8 M. For electrochemical Pb2+ monitoring, Wang et al. (2018b) developed MIL-101 (Cr-BDC)-modified GCE octahedral crystals that were inexpensive and effective. The MIL-101(Cr)/GCE was used to detect Pb2+ via differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV). Having a LOD of 5.0 × 10−10 nM, the electrochemical sensor responded linearly to Pb2+ in liquids with concentrations varying from 1.0 × 10−9 to 1.0 × 10−6 M (R2 0.999). This approach proved successful in detecting Pb2+ in real water samples, with considerable recovery from numerous spiked samples.

In another research, [Zn2(L)(IPA)(H2O)] as a novel porous MOF was developed by Liu et al. (2021). Utilizing a functionalized sulfonylcalix[4]arene (L1) and isophthalic acid (H2IPA), 2DMF2MeOH3H2O(Zn-L-IPA) was created (DMF = N,N′-dimethylformamide). Because of its pore shape and size, Zn-L-IPA was extremely preferential for the absorption of several EPs such as BPA, bisphenol F (BPF), and bisphenol S (BPS). Zn-L-IPA@GP/GCE was a rapid, ultrasensitive electrochemical sensing and highly selective platform that utilized Zn-L-IPA to detect and assimilate bisphenol guests (graphite powder (GP), GCE). Utilizing the Zn-L-IPA@GP/GCE electrode, the exceedingly low LODs for BPA and BPF were up to 3.46 and 0.17 nM, correspondingly. Moreover, using the B3LYP function and density functional theory, the “recognition and adsorption” mechanism was discovered. Using a MOF-based porous material, this study discovered a promising approach for targeted absorption and detection of hazardous bisphenols.

The diameter of the NPs is less than 1 m, generally between 1 and 100 nm. Furthermore, the size of MNPs has a significant impact on their characteristics, which are particularly important in contrast to bulk materials. The specific surface areas of NPs increase as particle size decreases. Furthermore, as the fraction of bulk atoms and surface increases, the surface or interfacial properties of NPs become extremely relevant. MNMs significantly improve electrochemical sensing performance due to their reduced diameters and large surface surfaces. Electrochemical signals or responses are often feeble due to the smaller surface areas of unaltered electrodes. As per experiments, utilizing MNPs as electrode surface moderators significantly enhances the electrodes' effective surface region. Increased electrode surface area may improve electrochemical signals promptly since electrochemical signals are related to electrode surface area. MNMs also have the potential to combine with other NMs (CNTs, metal and metal oxide NPs, and so on) to create composites with synergistic capabilities. The electrical conductivity of the surface is substantially elevated as a result of the synergistic impacts of the respective NMs, and the enhanced electrical conductivity speeds up the kinetics of the electron transfer method and minimizes the over-voltage of electrochemical operations. MNPs' magnetic features also help to improve the efficiency of specimen pre-treatment and extraction, as well as control targeted motions. As a result, analytical chemists have given MNPs-based electro-chemical biosensors serious consideration.

For electrochemical detection of dimethoate, Gan and colleagues designed a disposable enzyme biosensor based on a magnetic composite NMs enhanced screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) (Wang, 2011). The researchers manufactured AChE-coated Fe3O4/Au MNPs (Fe3O4/Au-AChE) and evaluated Fe3O4/Au-AChE absorption on the interface of an SPCE modulated by a CNTs/nano-ZrO2/Prussian blue(PB)/Nafion (NF) composite membrane using an external magnetic field. As a result, a biosensor was developed (SPCE|CNTs/ZrO2/PB/NF| Fe3O4/Au-AChE). The enzyme biosensor had a linear relationship with dimethoate concentrations in the range of 1.0 × 10−3 to 10 ng/mL as well as a LOD of 5.6 × 10−4 ng/mL (S/N = 3) at a pH of 7.5 KNO3 solutions, according to the results. Furthermore, the Chinese cabbage recovery rate was found to be between 88 and 105%. To assess chlorpyrifos residuals, Wang and colleagues constructed a high-performance biosensor centered on AChE, along with a composite membrane composed of MNP Fe3O4 and Gr modified SPE (Wang et al., 2011). Several approaches such as CV and DPV were used to describe the AChE/− Fe3O4/Gr/SPE, revealing that the biosensor has a lot of excellent characteristics such as greater consistency, reproducibility, and sensitivity. Based on the ideal condition, the LOD was 0.02 ng/L and the AChE suppression rate was proportional to chlorpyrifos doses in the range of 0.05 to 100 g/L. Their biosensor displayed improved precision, regeneration, and repeatability, making it suitable for detecting traces of chlorpyrifos residue in plants. With a recovery rate varied from 91.14 to 108.3%, this novel method was more accurate at detecting chlorpyrifos in vegetable specimens. Similarly, Zhang and colleagues announced the development of an AChE biosensor for detecting organophosphorus herbicides (chlopyrifos and methamidophos) (Zhang et al., 2020). On the SPCE surface, they used mesoporous carbon-CS (OMC-CS)/Fe3O4-CS to develop their biosensor. The film was modified to contain AChE in order to develop an AChE biosensor (AChE/OMC-CS/Fe3O4-CS/SPCE). CS was employed as a dispersion in order to scatter Fe3O4 and ordered mesoporous carbon (OMC). OMC and Fe3O4 have also been utilized to enhance electrochemical responses. CV was utilized to examine the electrochemical efficiency of AChE/OMC-CS/Fe3O4-CS/SPCE preliminary to the detection of pesticide. The analysis deliberates that the CS has the ability to effectively disseminate OMC and Fe3O4 and adhere them to the electrode surface. Furthermore, Fe3O4 and OMC have substantial synergic activity in electron transport. As a result, the sensitivity, durability, linear response range, fast reaction time, and LOD of the AChE biosensor for pesticide detection improved. The biosensor for AChE/OMC-CS/Fe3O4-CS/SPCE could also be used to detect specific materials (cabbage, rape & lettuce).

PET sensors are made up of molecules that have a fluorophore component for light emission and an ionophore portion for interacting with heavy metal ions (Fig. S10) (Burratti et al., 2021). When the system is devoid of pollutants, the transit of an electron across the ionophore state of energy to the excited fluorophore state mitigates the radiative transition across the two phases of the fluorophore component, so the emission intensity after optical excitation is weak. Because interaction with the cation stabilizes the ionophore's energy state, the probability of a radiative transition is increased during contamination by metal cations owing to the electron transport from the ionophore to the fluorophore being suppressed (Fig. S10). Two other processes that boost or reduce fluorescence strength are chelating quenching fluorescence (CHQF) and chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF). Furthermore, EPs such as heavy metal ions may cause emission units to cluster, and if the clustering is quite sufficient, the light generated might vanish entirely. The behavior differs depending on whether the ionophore moiety represents an electron donor or an electron acceptor species. When a fluorophore has an electron-donating ionophore coupled with an electron-accepting group, light illumination produces an intramolecular charge transfer from the donor to the acceptor. When an electron donor group within the fluorophore reacts with a cation, a blue tint in the absorption and emission spectra is expected. The metal cation weakens this group's electron-donating property, making the excited state more unstable than the ground state. The electron-attraction characteristic of an acceptor group increases when a cation interacts with it; as a result, the cation stabilizes the excited state more effectively than the ground state, resulting in a redshift in the emission and absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of NMs based biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The inductive component, transduction component, and signal processing and output system compose up an NMs based biosensing device. As it may conduct an electro-oxidation reaction with exceptional selectivity and sensitivity on its interface, the transduction component is the most critical component (Bucur et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2018; Rani and Shanker, 2018). The potential change, electrical volume, electrical conductance, impedance, the capacitance was all used in NMs based biosensors. Chemically selective scanning tunneling microscopes and scanning electrochemical microscopes were developed using a blend of microscopy and electrochemical ideas. Fig. S11 Illustrates the simplified illustration of the role of nanosensors in monitoring agroecosystems (Sharma et al., 2021).

In a research, Sudha et al. (2021) formed a convenient one-step chemical process for producing Copper (I) oxide (Cu2O), copper (II) oxide (CuO), and CuNa2(OH)4 crystal frameworks. SEM micrographs were used to explore the microstructural information of nanostructures. Fig. 6 a, c, e illustrates SEM pictures of Cu2O, CuO, and CuNa2(OH)4 NPs, respectively, while Fig. 6 b, d, f shows the relevant EDX findings. According to SEM micrographs, the particles were coarsely collected with uniform morphologies. The EDX analysis only showed the elements that were discovered in the specimens' spectra, not any extra contaminants or secondary compounds. According to the EDX results, the generated nanoparticles only include O and Cu ions in the same ratio percentage as stipulated at the period of research (Fig. 6 b, d).

Furthermore, they also investigated the impact of intensity on the efficacy of NMs based biosensors for EPs detection. The electrocatalytic sensitivity of CuO/GCE towards the detecting varied SO3 2+ concentrations (0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, and 15.0 mM) has been shown in Fig. S12 (a) (Sudha et al., 2021). CuO/GCE has been shown to have substantial electrocatalytic efficiency for SO3 2+ sensing. The analytical variables sensitivity and LOD determined to be 48.5 A cm2 mM−1 and 2.23 μM (LOD = 3/S; is the basic error and S is the sensitivity) in Fig. S12 (b), respectively, with an R2 of 0.9953. Furthermore, the DPV approach was used to investigate the efficacy of CuO/GCE for the identification of SO3 2+. The SO3 2+ oxidation spike was identified at 0.47 V using the DPV method, as illustrated in Fig. S12 (c). When the concentration of SO3 2+ was elevated from 0.005 to 15 mM, the oxidation current rises. The linear spectra, LOD, and sensitivities were reported to be 0.005–15 mM, 1.42 M, and 29.93 A cm2 mM−1 (R2 = 0.9906), accordingly, through the calibration curve illustrated in Fig. S12 (d). y = 29.93C (SO3 2+) + y = 29.93C (SO3 2+) + y = 29.93C (SO3 2+) + y = 29. (8.9). It's worth noting that the CuO/GCE-based sensor's performance exhibits superior accuracy and real-time analysis.

Due to the perceived toxicological impacts of NMs on human health and the environment, the huge nanotechnology sector and its expanding application in every discipline is also a subject of concern. While utilizing the NMs based biosensors, the usage of NMs has also increased and the discharge of these NMs into the ecosystem depletes the elegance and vibrancy of our planet's atmosphere. NMs that have been exposed to the ecosystem in any way have the potential to detour agricultural fields and water supplies, impacting soil fertility and water quality (Shah et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). For example, titanium oxide (TiO2) NPs in sunscreen could disintegrate into water reservoirs and be utilized to irrigate plants and crops, resulting in NPs bioaccumulating in the food chain and being passed down from generation to generation. Because of their small size, nanomaterials invade the human body through a variety of pathways, including ingestion, skin infiltration, and inhalation. Once in the bloodstream, these specific NPs may cause a variety of neurological abnormalities as well as potentially serious health problems like cardiac arrest and kidney failure (Justino et al., 2017; Rapini and Marrazza, 2017; Reddy Yadav et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2017). Also, the presence of NMs in the Earth's atmosphere could affect a variety of environmental processes, including the formation of dust clouds. These are made up of particulate debris and carbon nanosized components that are produced by the combustion of fossil fuels, plastic waste, soot-containing industrial fume and other sources. Brown clouds comprising smoke and carbon compounds might settle on glaciers, causing them to absorb more sunlight and melt. A drop in stratospheric temperature is another effect of NMs on the environment. This is because NMs, in combination with hydrogen in the environment, ascend to the stratosphere, causing stratospheric cooling, enhanced heterogeneous chemistry, and ozone layer depletion. NMs in the ecosystem is not only dangerous to humans, but they are also dangerous to other species. As a result, the discharge of NMs into Earth's atmosphere must be regulated as soon as possible in order to sustain our ecosystem's equilibrium and protect the well-being of all species on the planet.

Along with the NMs challenges, other technological obstacles, such as the inability to detect tiny molecules, recyclability, and adequate reliability for repeated measurements, obstruct the design and fabrication of NMs based biosensors. The most pertinent issue is whether NMs-based biosensors have enough specificity for use in real-world samples, that often comprise an unknown number of non-target molecules. Another key challenge is sensitivity, that is regulated by the width of the sensing layer. The detecting surface might well be accessible if the sensing layer is too thin, lowering the signal-to-noise ratio and decreasing sensitivity. Owing to the massive background signal in clinical specimens, cross-reaction, signal readouts and specificity could be particularly difficult to detect analytes (like pesticides, dyes, circulating cancer cells). One of the technological obstacles to the commercialization of NMs-based biosensors for environmental remediation is the huge amount and diversity of environmental contaminants. This wide range of contaminants/EPs has a direct impact on the monitoring and detecting activities and issues that come alongside the marketplace for field analytical techniques. As the market for single EP detection is limited, the most effective biosensor systems would either exchange a range of biological recognition components to detect a spectrum of related substances or be generic able to detect the existence of a number of EPs and their related compounds. Although, at this time, due to a lack of research on NMs-based biosensors, these biosensors are not well-suited for the detection of a variety of substances classified as dangerous environmental pollutants.

The emergence of EPs in the environment continues to cause more serious issues. Even at very low concentrations (ng/L), they can have a major influence on the ecosystem and human health. Source reduction and the replacement of EPs with sources that are less harmful and faster to eliminate from the ecosystem could help to minimize the influence of EPs on the environment and human health, although this is not always possible. However, despite tremendous progress in the detection of EPs using NMs based biosensors have been made, these sensors are currently in their infancy when it comes to being used in real-world applications. In laboratories, the proportions and qualities of specific EPs in several samples are known, but not in-field analysis. The results obtained in the research lab are challenging to validate by the findings obtained from real samples due to a variety of environmental factors. It is vital to obtain high selectivity, sensitivity and stability of biosensor platforms while designing sophisticated NMs based biosensors. When developing novel NMs, there are various factors to address, including shape and size regulated biosynthesis. The bi or tri-metallic NMs optimization and designs to obtain the greatest electrochemical active sites to enhance stability and specificity, functional macromolecular identification to improve analyte specificity, the relationship between NMs composition, structure, and reactivity to develop innovative NMs with high selectivity and most important the layout of nanometric interfaces provide an excellent framework for outstanding electrocatalytic characteristics. Additionally, the size, thickness, design, structure, and synthesis method of NMs based biosensor platforms are all essential factors in increasing their efficiency. Combining identification components with functionalized NMs will almost certainly raise the complexity, price, and time of a biosensor, particularly since recognition components will be less distinguishable. While biosensors based on NMs for the detection of EPs have made significant progress, new prospects and developments are evolving. Combining recently developed recognition components (peptide aptamers, nanobodies and so on) using functional materials/NM would open up new EPs monitoring and sensor effectiveness possibilities. Incorporating field-deployable gadgets with sensing devices, on the other hand, utilizing 3D printing technology to perform major on-site operations, may improve the sensor's repeatability and durability. However, due to a lack of significant coordination efforts between many research and engineering domains, direct analytical evaluations of the manufactured NMs based biosensors in the conventional ecological and food monitoring sectors are a further milestone. As a result, future endeavors should be solely focused on overcoming the aforementioned challenges. Furthermore, there are few literatures on measuring other sorts of EPs on a regular basis, hence greater emphasis is needed. With continued research, the advancements of biosensor platforms centered on NMs are predicted to be understood, allowing appropriate directions to be adopted in the near future to provide a better living for society.
